camp-three-falls.text: Solid traditional Scout camp - my son loved the horsemanship badge. Hot afternoons and rustic latrines, standard Scout fare.

lost-valley-scout-reservation.author: Scoutmaster
lost-valley-scout-reservation.text: The remoteness is the feature - no light pollution, coyotes at night, and a staff that leans into backcountry skills. The dirt road in rattles your teeth.

mataguay-scout-ranch.author: Eagle Scout alum
mataguay-scout-ranch.text: Earned half my Eagle badges there. Dusty, hot, wonderful - the pool at 3pm is salvation.

wente-scout-reservation.author: Scoutmaster
wente-scout-reservation.text: Waterskiing at Scout camp - Wente's lake program is the draw and the staff run it safely and generously. Long dusty road in, epic week once there.

camp-menzies.author: Girl Scout mom
camp-menzies.text: My daughter came home with a pocketknife certification and a new spine. Affordable thanks to cookie credits, and the all-girl space matters.

camp-kinneret.author: Second-generation camp parent
camp-kinneret.text: I went as a kid and now my daughters go - same songs, same swim-twice-a-day rhythm, same happy exhaustion at pickup. Bus counselors set the tone from the first stop.

camp-kinneret.author: Parent of a 5-year-old
camp-kinneret.text: Wonderful with young first-timers. It is pricey for a day camp and popular weeks fill by February.

tumbleweed-day-camp.author: Westside parent
tumbleweed-day-camp.text: Polished operation - great communication, beautiful canyon campus, and my kids never once said they were bored. You pay westside prices for it.

tumbleweed-day-camp.author: Camper, age 9
tumbleweed-day-camp.text: Zipline, gaga, swim, repeat. Fridays have themes and everyone goes all out.

cali-camp.author: Topanga parent
cali-camp.text: The canyon setting is the star - oaks, dust, creek smell, happy kids. Counselors skew artsy and warm. The drive up the boulevard takes patience.

cali-camp.author: Parent
cali-camp.text: My kids adore it. Communication is a little loose compared to the corporate camps, which fits the vibe honestly.

tom-sawyer-camps.author: Pasadena parent
tom-sawyer-camps.text: Kids on horseback in the arroyo like it is 1926 - zero screens, tons of dirt, deep traditions. Multi-generation families are the norm, so spots vanish early.

tom-sawyer-camps.author: Alum
tom-sawyer-camps.text: The bandana ranking system and Friday campfires shaped my childhood. My kids wear the same bandanas now.

fitness-by-the-sea.author: Santa Monica parent
fitness-by-the-sea.text: Pure beach childhood - surf lessons, sand soccer, and counselors with big-sibling energy. Day-by-day signup saved our patchwork summer schedule.

fitness-by-the-sea.author: Parent
fitness-by-the-sea.text: Kids love it. It is loosely structured by design, so kids who need firm routine may drift.

malibu-makos-surf-camp.author: Parent of a new surfer
malibu-makos-surf-camp.text: Instructors read the ocean and each kid individually - my daughter caught her first unassisted wave in week one. Mornings are foggy and the water is cold; wetsuits provided.

outpost-summer-camps.author: San Diego parent
outpost-summer-camps.text: Fort building, whittling, wildlife encounters - it is the childhood I wanted for my kids. Staff handle quirky, high-energy kids with unusual skill.

watersports-camp-mission-bay.author: Parent of a sailor
watersports-camp-mission-bay.text: University-affiliated instructors and a fleet most yacht clubs would envy - my daughter went from nervous to skippering a dinghy in two weeks. Book spring, not June.

watersports-camp-mission-bay.author: Camper, age 12
watersports-camp-mission-bay.text: I wakeboarded behind a real boat and learned to windsurf. Sunburn is the only downside.

ocean-institute-camps.author: OC parent
ocean-institute-camps.text: My daughter dissected a squid and helped raise the sails on a 19th-century brig in the same week. Real educators, real vessels - not a daycare with an ocean theme.

roughing-it-day-camp.author: East Bay parent
roughing-it-day-camp.text: Horseback riding at day camp, every week, for every kid - that alone sells it. The organization is airline-level: precise buses, detailed progress notes.

roughing-it-day-camp.author: Parent
roughing-it-day-camp.text: Our kids thrived for years. It is among the priciest day camps in the Bay, and full-summer families get priority, which squeezed our two-week plan.

camp-edmo.author: SF parent
camp-edmo.text: The kindness curriculum is real - counselors coach friendship skills as intentionally as robotics. Sliding-scale pricing made a full summer possible for us.

camp-edmo.author: Parent
camp-edmo.text: Great values and fun projects, though quality varies a bit by site director. Our school-campus location lacked shade.

steve-and-kates-camp-ca.author: Working parent of two
steve-and-kates-camp-ca.text: Buy a pass pack, use days whenever - the flexibility is unmatched and unused days refund. My kids beeline to the sewing machines and never look back.

steve-and-kates-camp-ca.author: Parent
steve-and-kates-camp-ca.text: Genius model for independent kids. Kids who need adult-led structure can wander; staff supervise safety more than engagement.

trackers-bay-area.author: Oakland parent
trackers-bay-area.text: Archery, mud, and guild lore - my D&D-loving kid found his tribe. Instructors are outdoor educators first, entertainers second.

trackers-bay-area.author: Parent
trackers-bay-area.text: Wonderful curriculum; logistics (site changes, packed pickup lines) can be scrappy. Kids too busy tracking raccoons to notice.

avid4-adventure-bay-area.author: Peninsula parent
avid4-adventure-bay-area.text: By August my 8-year-old could belay-check her own harness and paddle a kayak straight. Professional gear and instruction justify the price for us.

coastal-camp-naturebridge.author: Marin parent
coastal-camp-naturebridge.text: Camp inside a national park with educators who teach school groups all year - the naturalist knowledge is a cut above. Fog is constant; pack layers, always.

slide-ranch-camp.author: Mill Valley parent
slide-ranch-camp.text: Goats, tidepools, and cliffside views - my kids talk about the chickens by name all year. Small sessions sell out almost instantly in spring.

hidden-villa-summer-camp.author: Peninsula parent
hidden-villa-summer-camp.text: There is history in this soil - the first interracial camp in America, and the inclusive spirit endures. My son fed pigs and learned where food comes from.

hidden-villa-summer-camp.author: Alum
hidden-villa-summer-camp.text: Hidden Villa summers taught me compost, courage, and community. The creek hike to the white barn is a core memory.

lawrence-hall-science-camps.author: Berkeley parent
lawrence-hall-science-camps.text: Taught by museum educators with actual collections to explore - my daughter handled a snake and built a working circuit in week one. Parking on the hill is its own science problem.

planet-bravo.author: LA parent
planet-bravo.text: The rare tech camp with actual camp spirit - costume days, tournaments, silly chants - alongside legit coding progression across summers.

destination-science.author: Parent of a 7-year-old
destination-science.text: My son built a robot dog and talked about it for a month. Very organized, very sweet staff - ideal for the under-10 science kid.

play-well-teknologies.author: Parent
play-well-teknologies.text: Motorized LEGO contraptions and genuinely engineering-minded instructors. Half days go fast and my son begged for the following week. It is LEGO all day - know your kid.

camp-carolina-brevard.author: Parent of two campers
camp-carolina-brevard.text: The freedom to choose activities made my son take real ownership of his summer. He came home talking about kayaking clinics and Pisgah hikes for weeks.

camp-carolina-brevard.author: Former camper turned counselor
camp-carolina-brevard.text: Genuinely wild and fun place with an outdoors-first culture. Cabins are rustic and the schedule can feel loose if your kid needs more structure.

camp-illahee.author: Third-generation Illahee mom
camp-illahee.text: My mother went here, I went here, and now my daughters go. The singing, the white uniforms on Sundays, the counselors who remember your kid year to year — it all still holds up.

camp-illahee.author: Camper, age 13
camp-illahee.text: Riding and the climbing wall were my favorites. The food is actually good and cabin chats at night are the best part.

keystone-camp.author: Parent of a first-time camper
keystone-camp.text: The directors knew my daughter's name on day one and sent thoughtful updates. A small camp where a shy kid can't fall through the cracks.

keystone-camp.author: Alum from the 1990s
keystone-camp.text: Sweet, traditional place that hasn't changed much — which is mostly good. Facilities are older, but the traditions and horse program are what you come for.

gwynn-valley-camp.author: Parent of a 7-year-old
gwynn-valley-camp.text: The gold standard for a first overnight camp. The farm, the grist mill, the kindness of the staff — my daughter felt safe and came home more independent.

gwynn-valley-camp.author: Parent of returning campers
gwynn-valley-camp.text: Worth the waitlist. It fills up almost a year ahead, so register early. Zero screens, lots of mud, very happy kids.

camp-high-rocks.author: Parent of a returning camper
camp-high-rocks.text: Small enough that every counselor knew our son. The canoeing progression is legit — he came home paddling whitewater and prouder of himself than we'd ever seen.

camp-high-rocks.author: Alum and former staff
camp-high-rocks.text: The mountaintop setting and the emphasis on character over competition make it special. Not the camp for a kid who wants big tournaments and flashy facilities.

green-river-preserve.author: Parent of a quirky naturalist
green-river-preserve.text: The first camp where my kid felt like the other campers were his people. Mentor hikes with naturalists are the heart of it — this is not a sports camp and doesn't pretend to be.

green-river-preserve.author: Teen camper
green-river-preserve.text: Loved the fly fishing and the night hikes. Food is farm-to-table-ish and good. Wish sessions were longer, and cell-free takes adjusting.

eagles-nest-camp.author: Parent of a camper and Hante teen
eagles-nest-camp.text: Crunchy in the best way — singing before meals, gardens, real backpacking. The teen adventure trips built more confidence than anything else we've tried.

eagles-nest-camp.author: Former camper
eagles-nest-camp.text: A warm, accepting place where artsy and outdoorsy kids mix. Cabins are open-air and rustic, so pack for cool mountain nights.

camp-chosatonga.author: Dad of a 12-year-old
camp-chosatonga.text: Old-school in a good way — knife safety, canoe strokes, manners at the table. Remote setting means limited communication home, which was harder on us than on him.

camp-merrie-woode.author: Alumna and current camp mom
camp-merrie-woode.text: The mountain, the lake, the ceremonies — it's a world of its own. Getting a spot is genuinely hard if you're not a legacy family, so plan years ahead.

camp-merrie-woode.author: Camper, age 14
camp-merrie-woode.text: Paddling to the far end of the lake and climbing Old Bald are rites of passage. Cabins are open-air and chilly at night but that's part of it.

camp-mondamin.author: Parent of a paddler
camp-mondamin.text: Mondamin turned my son into a genuinely skilled whitewater canoeist. The anti-hype, no-awards philosophy is rare and it works — but kids who need constant structure may drift.

camp-mondamin.author: Alum from the 1980s
camp-mondamin.text: Some of the best paddlers in the country trace their start here. Rustic cabins, cold lake, lifelong friends.

camp-green-cove.author: Mom of a two-summer camper
camp-green-cove.text: Green Cove trusts girls with real skills — my daughter rolled a kayak at 12 and led a hike at 14. Unpolished facilities, extraordinary outcomes.

camp-glen-arden.author: Parent of a returning camper
camp-glen-arden.text: Feels like a family — directors are hands-on and the older girls genuinely mentor the younger ones. Not flashy, deeply loved.

camp-ton-a-wandah.author: Parent of a first-timer
camp-ton-a-wandah.text: The one-week starter session was perfect for our nervous 7-year-old. She came home singing camp songs and asking for two weeks next year.

camp-ton-a-wandah.author: Alumna
camp-ton-a-wandah.text: Classic camp magic — campfires, mountain trail rides, tight cabin bonds. Facilities are dated in spots but the traditions carry it.

camp-blue-star.author: Parent from Atlanta
camp-blue-star.text: Blue Star is where Southern Jewish kids find their people. Friday night Shabbat on the hill is genuinely moving, and the friendships last decades.

camp-blue-star.author: Former camper
camp-blue-star.text: Huge camp with something for everyone, though that size means you have to put yourself out there. The lake and blob never get old.

camp-judaea.author: Parent of two campers
camp-judaea.text: The Israeli staff and cultural programming set it apart — my kids came home with Hebrew phrases and a real connection to their identity. Kosher kitchen handled allergies well.

camp-pinewood-nc.author: Miami parent
camp-pinewood-nc.text: Half the camp seems to be escaping the Florida heat with us. Nice mix of international kids, and the elective system kept our son engaged for four weeks.

camp-pinewood-nc.author: Camper, age 12
camp-pinewood-nc.text: Waterskiing every day was awesome. Cabins get crowded and the canteen line is long, but I want to go back.

camp-wayfarer.author: Parent of siblings
camp-wayfarer.text: Being able to send our son and daughter to the same camp with separate cabin lives was the selling point. Warm staff, beautiful grounds, strong communication.

camp-tekoa.author: Methodist youth leader
camp-tekoa.text: Tekoa punches way above its price. Kids from all backgrounds are welcomed, the lake toys are a blast, and the staff culture is famously loving.

camp-tekoa.author: Parent
camp-tekoa.text: Great value church camp. Cabins are simple and one week goes fast, but my kids ask for it every summer.

camp-pinnacle-nc.author: Parent of an 11-year-old
camp-pinnacle-nc.text: Nicer facilities than most mountain camps — real beds, good food — while still getting kids properly dirty on bikes and trails.

camp-pinnacle-nc.author: Camper
camp-pinnacle-nc.text: The bike trails and the lake were the best. Some activities filled up fast so I didn't always get my first pick.

camp-grier.author: Parent of a mountain biker
camp-grier.text: The new trail networks around Old Fort make this a bike kid's dream. Unpretentious, welcoming, and priced like camps used to be.

lutheridge.author: Lutheran parent
lutheridge.text: Three generations of our family have sung the same table graces here. Counselors are seminary-bound sweethearts and the price is right.

camp-cedar-cliff.author: Parent
camp-cedar-cliff.text: Solid biblical teaching and counselors who clearly love the kids. My son's favorite part was paintball and the giant swing.

camp-hollymont.author: Parent of an 8-year-old
camp-hollymont.text: Clean, organized, and nurturing — a soft landing for a first sleepaway summer. Photos posted daily kept the grandparents happy.

ridge-haven.author: PCA youth pastor
ridge-haven.text: Our church group returns every summer. Sound teaching, sweet fellowship, and enough adventure to wear the kids out completely.

camp-henry-nc.author: Parent from Asheville
camp-henry-nc.text: Lake Logan might be the prettiest camp setting in the state. Small, kind, and my kids' counselors felt like older siblings.

camp-celo.author: Parent of a returning camper
camp-celo.text: Sixty kids, goats, a swimming hole, and silence before meals. The waitlist is real — families stay in the fold for a decade.

camp-spring-creek.author: Parent of a dyslexic camper
camp-spring-creek.text: The only place where reading help ever felt like a privilege instead of a punishment. Tiny, expensive, and worth every penny for the right kid.

ymca-camp-watia.author: Parent
ymca-camp-watia.text: Newer cabins, generous financial aid, and rafting on the Nantahala — a great value overnight camp for the mountains.

camp-lurecrest.author: Charlotte parent
camp-lurecrest.text: The lake setting is spectacular — my kids live for the ski boats. Worship is upbeat and the counselors invest all year, not just at camp.

camp-pisgah-girl-scouts.author: Girl Scout mom
camp-pisgah-girl-scouts.text: Affordable, sweet, and genuinely outdoorsy. Facilities are basic scout-camp standard, but the horse program surprised us with its quality.

camp-cheerio.author: Winston-Salem parent
camp-cheerio.text: Cheerio is a rite of passage around here — kids of kids of campers. Sessions sell out the morning registration opens, so set an alarm.

camp-cheerio.author: Camper, age 13
camp-cheerio.text: Cool mountain air, horses, and the best counselors. The dance on the last night is everything.

ymca-camp-harrison.author: Charlotte parent
ymca-camp-harrison.text: Easy drive from Charlotte, strong values programming, and scholarships that make camp possible for a lot of families.

ymca-camp-hanes.author: Parent of two
ymca-camp-hanes.text: Our kids started with day camp and graduated to overnight weeks. Counselors greet returning campers by name a year later.

ymca-camp-weaver.author: Working parent
ymca-camp-weaver.text: Feels like resident camp packed into a day — real lake, real trails. Drop-off lines get long in peak weeks.

ymca-camp-kanata.author: Raleigh parent
ymca-camp-kanata.text: The mini-week option eased our 7-year-old into overnight camp perfectly. Counselors are high-energy and communication is excellent.

raven-knob-scout-reservation.author: Eagle Scout alum
raven-knob-scout-reservation.text: Raven Knob summers made my whole scouting career — the campfire ceremonies under the knob are burned into my memory.

camp-caraway.author: Baptist parent
camp-caraway.text: Solid week of fun and faith without breaking the bank. Cabins are simple; the counselors are college guys who really engage.

camp-mundo-vista.author: Parent
camp-mundo-vista.text: My daughter came home singing and asking about missionaries. Sweet, affordable, old-fashioned church camp.

quaker-lake-camp.author: Quaker parent
quaker-lake-camp.text: Kind, unhurried, and rooted in simplicity. The evening vespers by the lake stay with kids long after summer.

camp-chestnut-ridge.author: Durham parent
camp-chestnut-ridge.text: The farm animals and forest play are a childhood in miniature. Faith content is warm and light-handed — kids of all backgrounds fit in.

camp-oak-hill.author: Parent
camp-oak-hill.text: A hidden gem for Triangle families — close to home but feels away. Strong values, good horsemanship program.

camp-willow-run.author: Parent of a returning camper
camp-willow-run.text: Sleeping in a boxcar is the hook, but the waterfront staff and worship nights are why my kids return. Book early — weeks sell out.

camp-monroe-nc.author: Presbyterian grandparent
camp-monroe-nc.text: Three generations of our family have gone. Humble facilities, huge heart, and a price any family can manage.

camp-sea-gull.author: Parent of a sailor
camp-sea-gull.text: The seamanship ladder is the real thing — my son can dock a motorboat better than I can. Enormous camp, superbly organized.

camp-sea-gull.author: Alum
camp-sea-gull.text: Salt, sails, and lifelong friends. Its size is a feature and a bug: endless options, but you're one of many hundreds.

camp-seafarer.author: Mom of a helmswoman
camp-seafarer.text: Seafarer girls carry themselves differently — capable on the water and proud of it. Registration is competitive; returning families get priority.

camp-caroline.author: Parent
camp-caroline.text: Sweet little waterfront camp with a family feel. Not fancy, but the price and the people keep us coming back.

camp-albemarle-nc.author: Coastal NC parent
camp-albemarle-nc.text: Sound-side sunsets and salt marsh exploring — a coastal childhood in one week. Simple bunkhouses, big memories.

camp-trinity-salter-path.author: Episcopal parent
camp-trinity-salter-path.text: Ocean on one side, sound on the other — the setting does half the ministry. Gentle, inclusive, and beloved across the diocese.

betsy-jeff-penn-4h-camp.author: Parent
betsy-jeff-penn-4h-camp.text: A classic first sleepaway camp — one week, kind staff, and real skills like archery and paddling. Great value.

millstone-4h-camp.author: 4-H parent
millstone-4h-camp.text: Old-school and proud of it — no AC in some cabins, but the staff spirit and lake time win kids over fast.

eastern-4h-center.author: Parent
eastern-4h-center.text: The sound-side setting is something special — my daughter saw bears, otters, and a bald eagle in one week.

camp-virginia.author: Richmond dad
camp-virginia.text: Feels like the camp I went to in the 80s, in the best way. Small enough that the directors know every boy's story.

camp-mont-shenandoah.author: Alumna and camp mom
camp-mont-shenandoah.text: The river, the mountain, the songs — it's unchanged in the ways that matter. My daughter wears her camp ring like I wore mine.

camp-mont-shenandoah.author: Camper, age 12
camp-mont-shenandoah.text: Swimming in the Cowpasture beats any pool. Evening programs and banquet are the highlights of my whole year.

camp-strawderman.author: DC-area parent
camp-strawderman.text: No frills, all heart: horses every day, plays in a stone outdoor theater, letters home on paper. My daughter adores it.

camp-strawderman.author: Alumna
camp-strawderman.text: Time capsule of a camp — swimming hole, mountain rides, zero screens. Facilities are genuinely rustic, so know your kid.

camp-horizons-va.author: Parent of two campers
camp-horizons-va.text: Counselors from all over the world give it a fun, big-hearted feel. Great mix of adventure and low-key farm charm.

nature-camp-vesuvius.author: Alum, now a biologist
nature-camp-vesuvius.text: Nature Camp made half the field scientists I know. Two weeks of stream ecology and salamanders that changed my life's direction.

nature-camp-vesuvius.author: Parent
nature-camp-vesuvius.text: For the kid who owns field guides, there is nowhere better. Spots are scarce and alumni families fill many of them.

triple-c-camp.author: Local parent
triple-c-camp.text: The camp everyone in town uses and loves. Kids come home wet, dirty, and happy. Popular weeks fill by early spring.

westview-on-the-james.author: Methodist parent
westview-on-the-james.text: Riverfront trail rides and campfire worship — simple and lovely. The staff treats every kid like the favorite.

camp-piankatank.author: Parent
camp-piankatank.text: River camp done right — my kids sailed, fished, and came home asking big questions in the best way.

ymca-camp-kekoka.author: Parent
ymca-camp-kekoka.text: Tidewater charm and a caring staff. My daughter's favorite memory was pulling up the crab pots before breakfast.

chanco-on-the-james.author: Episcopal parent
chanco-on-the-james.text: Sunset eucharist over the James is unforgettable. Inclusive, joyful, and priced so the whole parish can go.

camp-skimino.author: Girl Scout mom
camp-skimino.text: Classic scout camp — tents, badges, s'mores. Tidewater humidity is real, but the girls barely notice.

camp-highroad.author: NoVA parent
camp-highroad.text: Half an hour from the suburbs but feels like real country. The horse weeks are the first to fill.

ymca-camp-silver-beach.author: Virginia Beach parent
ymca-camp-silver-beach.text: Bay sunsets and beach games every night. It books out fast because nothing else around offers real saltwater camp life.

occohannock-on-the-bay.author: Eastern Shore parent
occohannock-on-the-bay.text: Our local treasure. Kids catch crabs off the dock and sing grace before supper — camp the way it used to be.

triple-r-ranch.author: Horse-crazy camper's mom
triple-r-ranch.text: Real barn responsibility, not just pony rides. My daughter mucked stalls happily — a miracle — and grew in her faith too.

camp-william-b-snyder.author: Cub Scout parent
camp-william-b-snyder.text: Well-organized intro to camping for little guys. Facilities are newer than most scout camps by a mile.

shrine-mont-camps.author: Episcopal parent
shrine-mont-camps.text: St. George's camp weeks are a diocesan institution — my kids count down to them. The outdoor cathedral is breathtaking.

camp-overlook-va.author: Valley parent
camp-overlook-va.text: The view alone is worth it. Small, faithful, and my kids' counselors still send them birthday cards.

brethren-woods.author: Brethren parent
brethren-woods.text: Peace-church values in action — cooperation over competition. My anxious kid thrived in the small, kind community.

caroline-furnace-camp.author: Lutheran parent
caroline-furnace-camp.text: Feels like summer church in the best way — muddy boots, belly laughs, and grace at every meal.

camp-bethel-va.author: Roanoke-area parent
camp-bethel-va.text: Generations of our church have grown up at Bethel. Kind counselors, creek mud, and songs you never forget.

camp-alta-mons.author: Parent
camp-alta-mons.text: The Stiles Falls hike is a rite of passage. Homey, affordable, and staffed with genuinely caring people.

camp-blue-ridge-montebello.author: Adventist parent
camp-blue-ridge-montebello.text: Clean mountain air, kind staff, and Sabbath rest built into the rhythm. The horsemanship track is excellent.

holiday-lake-4h-center.author: 4-H parent
holiday-lake-4h-center.text: Classic county-camp week: teen counselors, lake swims, and new friends from three counties over. Unbeatable price.

jamestown-4h-center.author: Parent
jamestown-4h-center.text: Sweet first-camp experience on the river. It's humid and the cabins are simple, but the counselors bring huge energy.

northern-virginia-4h-center.author: Fairfax parent
northern-virginia-4h-center.text: An honest, affordable classic — my suburban kid came home knowing how to shoot a bow and lead a cheer.

we-skelton-4h-center.author: Franklin County parent
we-skelton-4h-center.text: Lakefront camp at a price real families can pay. My kids' favorite week of every summer.

camp-easter-seals-ucp.author: Parent of a camper who uses a wheelchair
camp-easter-seals-ucp.text: Everything — the zip line, the pool, the trails — is genuinely accessible, not an afterthought. My daughter is a different kid there.

camp-roanoke.author: Roanoke parent
camp-roanoke.text: City-run and terrific — real hikes, real campfires, tiny price. The old CCC lodges give it soul.

camp-happyland.author: DC grandparent
camp-happyland.text: They made camp possible for our family when nothing else was. The staff pour love into these kids.

camp-motorsport.author: Parent of a kart-obsessed kid
camp-motorsport.text: Nothing else like it on the East Coast. Real track time daily, and the mechanical classes were a sneaky education.

camp-motorsport.author: Camper, age 13
camp-motorsport.text: Driving every day was amazing. Non-track hours are more basic camp stuff, which is fine but not why you go.

burgundy-farm-summer-day-camp.author: Alexandria parent
burgundy-farm-summer-day-camp.text: Goats and creek mud five minutes from the highway. The counselors skew arty and kind — our kind of place.

congressional-camp.author: NoVA parent of two
congressional-camp.text: Polished operation with great swim instruction. It's pricey and the bus routes fill early, but the kids adore it.

camp-hilbert.author: Richmond JCC parent
camp-hilbert.text: Warm Jewish community feel with a real lake and real trees, not a parking-lot camp. Friday song sessions are the best.

camp-chatuga.author: Parent of returning campers
camp-chatuga.text: Run by the same family for generations and it shows — unfussy, personal, and full of traditions my kids quote all year.

camp-chatuga.author: Alum
camp-chatuga.text: Not a fancy camp, and that's the point. The lake, the counselor skits, the crazy relays — pure childhood.

ymca-camp-greenville.author: Upstate parent
ymca-camp-greenville.text: Sunrise at Pretty Place alone justifies the tuition. Strong values programming and counselors who clearly love the mountain.

ymca-camp-greenville.author: Former camper
ymca-camp-greenville.text: Waterfalls, high ropes, and chapel above the clouds. Cabins are old-school but you won't care.

ymca-camp-thunderbird.author: Charlotte parent
ymca-camp-thunderbird.text: The waterski instruction is the best in the region — my daughter went from zero to slalom in two summers. Registration is a sprint.

ymca-camp-thunderbird.author: Former counselor
ymca-camp-thunderbird.text: Huge, happy, high-energy camp. With 500 kids a session it hums like a machine, which is impressive if a little less intimate.

camp-gravatt.author: Aiken parent
camp-gravatt.text: Kind counselors, cypress-dark lake, candlelight chapel — my kids leave calmer and taller somehow.

asbury-hills-camp.author: Methodist parent
asbury-hills-camp.text: Creek hikes and campfire worship in the mountains for under a thousand dollars — the best value in upstate camping.

camp-fellowship-sc.author: Presbyterian parent
camp-fellowship-sc.text: Big lake, small camp, huge heart. The counselors remembered my son's fishing obsession from last summer.

bethelwoods-camp.author: Rock Hill parent
bethelwoods-camp.text: Close to home, kind staff, fair price. Facilities are older but well cared for, and the kids never notice.

camp-kinard.author: Lutheran parent
camp-kinard.text: Small and sweet — every counselor knew every camper by day two. Old-fashioned in all the right ways.

camp-longridge.author: Columbia parent
camp-longridge.text: My kids call it the fun church camp — ski boats all afternoon, worship band at night. They beg to go back.

camp-mccall.author: Baptist dad
camp-mccall.text: Hard to beat: a week in the mountains, strong male role models, and a price under most day camps.

camp-wabak.author: Girl Scout mom
camp-wabak.text: Affordable and empowering — girls run the show. Platform tents and mountain nights make it feel like real camping.

camp-low-country.author: Charleston-area parent
camp-low-country.text: Riding horses under mossy oaks is pure lowcountry magic. Hot in July, but the pool and the friendships carry it.

camp-old-indian.author: Scoutmaster
camp-old-indian.text: Strong program areas and deep traditions. It's an old camp — some facilities creak — but the staff spirit is unmatched.

camp-barstow.author: Scout alum
camp-barstow.text: Learned to sail on Lake Murray at Barstow and never stopped. The waterfront staff run a tight, fun ship.

camp-bob-cooper.author: 4-H parent
camp-bob-cooper.text: An institution for midlands families — my father camped here too. Big lake, big skies, small price.

clemson-outdoor-lab.author: Parent of a deaf camper
clemson-outdoor-lab.text: Staff who sign, peers who get it, and a real lakefront camp week. One of the few places my son is just one of the kids.

adirondack-camp.author: Parent of two returning campers
adirondack-camp.text: The lakefront setting is unbeatable and the staff genuinely know every kid. Our shy daughter came home singing camp songs and begging for the full season.

adirondack-camp.author: Alum from the 2000s
adirondack-camp.text: Wildly strong traditions and an international camper mix you don't find everywhere. Cabins are rustic and the price has crept up, but the magic is real.

ymca-camp-chingachgook.author: Parent of a first-time camper
ymca-camp-chingachgook.text: Incredible value for a Lake George waterfront. Counselors were warm and communicative, and our son learned to sail in one week.

ymca-camp-chingachgook.author: Former counselor
ymca-camp-chingachgook.text: Genuinely well-run with great trip options into the High Peaks. Sessions turn over fast, so friendships can feel short for kids who only do one week.

forest-lake-camp.author: Parent of a returning camper
forest-lake-camp.text: Old-school in the best way — kids off screens, in the lake, on the trails. Communication from the office was solid all summer.

forest-lake-camp.author: Camper, age 14
forest-lake-camp.text: The land is huge and the color war is awesome. Food is just okay and the cabins are pretty bare-bones, but that's kind of the point.

camp-lincoln-ny.author: Parent of a third-year camper
camp-lincoln-ny.text: No trophies, no rankings, just boys building real competence in canoes and on mountains. The small size means every counselor knows your kid.

camp-lincoln-ny.author: Alum and former counselor
camp-lincoln-ny.text: The hiking and canoe trip program is the heart of it. Not the camp for a kid who needs constant electronics or big-league sports.

camp-whippoorwill.author: Parent of a returning camper
camp-whippoorwill.text: My daughter came back confident, muddy, and full of trip stories. The riding program is a quiet gem.

camp-whippoorwill.author: Alum
camp-whippoorwill.text: A beautifully simple place. Just know it's rustic — think outhouses on trips and cold lake swims, which some girls love and some don't.

camp-pok-o-maccready.author: Parent of a horse-obsessed camper
camp-pok-o-maccready.text: The riding and the mountain trips set it apart — my kid summited real High Peaks at 11. Facilities are old but lovingly kept.

camp-pok-o-maccready.author: Alum from the 90s
camp-pok-o-maccready.text: Pok-O is tradition on tradition — same songs, same mountains, families going back generations. It gets in your blood.

camp-treetops.author: Parent of a two-summer camper
camp-treetops.text: Kids milk goats before breakfast and climb High Peaks by August. It's expensive and the full-season commitment is real, but the growth is remarkable.

camp-treetops.author: Alum
camp-treetops.text: The farm work and trail crew shaped who I am. This is a values-driven place — families looking for jet skis and tournaments should look elsewhere.

camp-baco.author: Parent of a longtime camper
camp-baco.text: Same families come back decade after decade and the owners know everyone by name. Full-summer-only is a big commitment for a first-timer.

camp-baco.author: Alum
camp-baco.text: Baco brothers are for life — the sing, the banquet, the lake at dusk. Sports matter here but nobody rides the bench.

camp-che-na-wah.author: Parent of two campers
camp-che-na-wah.text: My daughters count the days until camp all year. The traditions and the lifelong friendships are the whole point here.

camp-che-na-wah.author: Alum
camp-che-na-wah.text: Gorgeous lake and real sisterhood. It's a seven-week camp with a waitlist culture, so plan early and be ready for rustic bunks.

camp-regis-applejack.author: Parent of a first-time camper
camp-regis-applejack.text: Genuinely welcoming to all kinds of kids and families. The lake is stunning and the tone is kind rather than cliquey.

camp-regis-applejack.author: Alum
camp-regis-applejack.text: Laid-back, diverse, a little quirky. Facilities are older and Wi-Fi-free, which suited us fine.

tanager-lodge.author: Parent of an alum family
tanager-lodge.text: Fifty-some kids, no electricity in the cabins, and the strongest outdoor skills program we've seen. It books up through word of mouth.

tanager-lodge.author: Alum
tanager-lodge.text: Tanager taught me to paddle, portage, and love the woods. It is genuinely rugged — kids who need creature comforts will struggle.

camp-jeanne-darc.author: Parent of a returning camper
camp-jeanne-darc.text: Small enough that the directors know every girl. My daughter learned to waterski at eight and cried when we picked her up.

camp-jeanne-darc.author: Alum
camp-jeanne-darc.text: Sweet, traditional, and far north — the drive is long and the lake is cold, but the friendships last.

camp-chateaugay.author: Parent of two campers
camp-chateaugay.text: Great mix of structure and choice, and the waterfront program is top-notch. Kids from all over the world in the bunks.

camp-chateaugay.author: Camper, age 13
camp-chateaugay.text: Awesome lake days and evening activities. It's really far from everything, which makes visiting day a haul for parents.

camp-walden-ny.author: Parent of a returning camper
camp-walden-ny.text: Beautiful grounds and a real community feel — the sibling-style bonds between bunks are special. Not cheap, and spots go fast.

camp-walden-ny.author: Alum
camp-walden-ny.text: Loved my summers there. It skews social and sporty; a quiet artsy kid may need a minute to find their crew.

camp-southwoods.author: Parent of a nervous first-timer
camp-southwoods.text: They handled homesickness like pros and sent photos daily. Our kid signed up for double the time next summer.

camp-southwoods.author: Camper, age 12
camp-southwoods.text: The lake trampoline and league nights are the best. Counselors actually play with you instead of standing around.

camp-southwoods.author: Parent
camp-southwoods.text: Very polished operation, almost resort-like. If you want gritty old-school Adirondack camping this isn't quite that.

camp-tapawingo-ny.author: Parent of two daughters
camp-tapawingo-ny.text: Faith-filled without being preachy, and the girls come home with real outdoor skills and scripture songs alike.

camp-tapawingo-ny.author: Alum
camp-tapawingo-ny.text: Beautiful, simple, and screen-free. The one-week sessions fly by — wish we'd booked two.

camp-fowler.author: Parent
camp-fowler.text: Affordable, joyful, and grounded. The staff build a genuinely accepting community in just a week.

camp-fowler.author: Alum and counselor
camp-fowler.text: Fowler's table fellowship and no-frills cabins shaped my whole outlook. Food is homestyle and the lake is everything.

camp-turk.author: Parent of two campers
camp-turk.text: Astonishing value thanks to the Masonic subsidy. Old-fashioned in the best sense, with staff who return year after year.

camp-turk.author: Alum
camp-turk.text: Round Lake at sunrise is magic. Bunks and bathrooms show their age, but nobody seems to mind.

ymca-camp-gorham.author: Parent
ymca-camp-gorham.text: The ranch program made my daughter's summer — real trail rides in real mountains for Y-camp prices.

ymca-camp-gorham.author: Former counselor
ymca-camp-gorham.text: Beautiful lake and dedicated staff, though cabins fill up and turnover weeks can feel hectic.

camp-mark-seven.author: Parent of a deaf camper
camp-mark-seven.text: The first place my son was never the odd one out. Full ASL access at every activity, from tubing to campfire.

camp-mark-seven.author: Alum
camp-mark-seven.text: Deaf role models everywhere and a gorgeous lake. Sessions are short and demand is high, so register early.

skye-farm-camp.author: Parent
skye-farm-camp.text: Kind staff, simple cabins, honest prices. Our kids ask for their week at Skye Farm before anything else all summer.

skye-farm-camp.author: Former camper
skye-farm-camp.text: Small and homey with lovely vespers by the lake. Activities are more low-key than at bigger camps.

camp-little-notch.author: Parent
camp-little-notch.text: A whole lake to themselves and zero pressure to be anything but muddy and happy. The alumnae community keeps this place alive with love.

camp-little-notch.author: Alum
camp-little-notch.text: Platform tents, latrines, and lake swims — gloriously old-school, though girls used to plumbing may need a day to adjust.

eagle-island-camp.author: Parent
eagle-island-camp.text: An actual island, an actual national historic landmark, and scholarships that made it possible for us. Unreal setting.

eagle-island-camp.author: Alum volunteer
eagle-island-camp.text: Generations fought to reopen this place and it shows. Simple program, spectacular water, deep sisterhood.

dec-camp-colby.author: Parent of a nature-loving kid
dec-camp-colby.text: The best-kept secret in NY summers — a real Adirondack week for the price of a day camp. Spots vanish in minutes when registration opens.

dec-camp-colby.author: Former camper
dec-camp-colby.text: Learned to fly fish and ID trees, made fast friends. It's one week only and pretty basic bunkhouses.

dec-camp-pack-forest.author: Parent
dec-camp-pack-forest.text: My son came home talking about forest succession and begging for hiking boots. Unbeatable for the price.

dec-camp-pack-forest.author: Camper, age 14
dec-camp-pack-forest.text: Really cool hikes and the counselors know everything about animals. Less free time than regular camp.

camp-guggenheim.author: Parent
camp-guggenheim.text: A joyful, wholesome week — my teens came home closer to their faith and already planning next year with camp friends.

camp-guggenheim.author: Alum
camp-guggenheim.text: Guggenheim summers were the highlight of my teenage years. Simple cabins, stunning lake, lifelong friends.

beaver-camp.author: Parent
beaver-camp.text: Kind, servant-hearted staff and a gorgeous quiet lake. The price makes it possible for all three of our kids to go.

beaver-camp.author: Former camper
beaver-camp.text: Beaver Camp weeks were pure joy — tubing, cookouts, cabin devotions. Small enough that everyone belongs.

camp-aldersgate-ny.author: Parent
camp-aldersgate-ny.text: Three generations of our family have camped here. Unpretentious, faith-filled, and full of counselors who care.

camp-aldersgate-ny.author: Camper, age 12
camp-aldersgate-ny.text: The lake and horses are great. Cabins get chilly at night, bring a warm sleeping bag.

oswegatchie-educational-center.author: Parent of an FFA member
oswegatchie-educational-center.text: Leadership weeks here lit a fire in my daughter. Beautiful woods and a staff that treats kids like capable adults.

oswegatchie-educational-center.author: Alum
oswegatchie-educational-center.text: Canoeing, rifle range, and ag projects — a different flavor than sports camps, in a good way.

kamp-kiwanis.author: Parent
kamp-kiwanis.text: Our local Kiwanis club sponsored my son's week and the staff made him feel like a VIP. Forever grateful.

kamp-kiwanis.author: Volunteer
kamp-kiwanis.text: A mission-first camp that quietly changes lives every week of the summer.

camp-scully.author: Parent
camp-scully.text: Sliding-scale pricing and scholarships meant both kids could go. Simple, sweet, and close to home.

camp-scully.author: Alum
camp-scully.text: Generations of Troy and Albany kids learned to swim here. Nothing fancy, everything that matters.

clover-patch-camp.author: Parent of a camper with cerebral palsy
clover-patch-camp.text: The only week of the year my daughter is away from us — and she counts down to it. Staff are angels.

clover-patch-camp.author: Counselor
clover-patch-camp.text: Adaptive swim, sensory rooms, accessible trails — everything designed so every camper participates fully.

falcon-camp-oh.author: Parent of two returning campers
falcon-camp-oh.text: Rare thing in Ohio — a real old-fashioned private camp. Kids pick their own activities and the staff genuinely know every child. Worth the drive from Cleveland.

falcon-camp-oh.author: Alum from the 1990s
falcon-camp-oh.text: Falcon shaped my summers. Sailing on Leesville, campfires, the same families coming back for generations. It's smaller and homier than the big-name East Coast camps.

falcon-camp-oh.author: First-time camp parent
falcon-camp-oh.text: Great two-week starter session, though it books up early and the price is a step above the church and Y camps nearby. Communication from directors was excellent.

ymca-camp-willson.author: Parent of a horse-crazy camper
ymca-camp-willson.text: The ranch program is the reason we come back — real barn chores and trail rides, not just a pony walk. Cabins are basic but the counselors are warm.

ymca-camp-willson.author: Former counselor
ymca-camp-willson.text: Classic Y camp energy, big song circles, tight-knit staff. Facilities are showing their age in spots but the kids never seem to notice.

ymca-camp-kern.author: Cincinnati parent
ymca-camp-kern.text: Our kids have done everything from mini camp to the ranch program. Huge variety, and registration is smooth. It can feel large, but counselors keep groups small.

ymca-camp-kern.author: Teen camper
ymca-camp-kern.text: River trips and the zipline are the best part. Food is standard camp food and the summer heat in the valley is real, but I count down the days every year.

ymca-camp-campbell-gard.author: Parent of a camper with special needs
ymca-camp-campbell-gard.text: They welcomed our son when other camps hesitated, and paired him with staff who understood him. Inclusion here isn't a slogan, it's how the place runs.

ymca-camp-campbell-gard.author: Longtime area parent
ymca-camp-campbell-gard.text: Sweet, unpretentious camp. Bunks are rustic and it books fast in early spring, but the price is fair and the kids come home filthy and happy.

ymca-camp-tippecanoe.author: Grandparent who sends three grandkids
ymca-camp-tippecanoe.text: Feels like camp did fifty years ago — woods, lake, horses, no screens. The remote location means a long drive for most families, but that's part of the charm.

ymca-camp-tippecanoe.author: Returning camper
ymca-camp-tippecanoe.text: Horseback and the giant swing are awesome. Cabins get hot in July and showers are a hike, but my cabin group was like family by Friday.

ymca-camp-y-noah.author: Akron working parent
ymca-camp-y-noah.text: We use day camp all summer and add an overnight week in July. Bus stops around the suburbs make logistics easy, and the staff turnover seems low for a Y camp.

ymca-camp-y-noah.author: Parent of a nervous first-timer
ymca-camp-y-noah.text: Counselors handled homesickness kindly. Weeks fill early and the popular horse add-on costs extra, which snuck up on us.

camp-wise.author: Third-generation camp family
camp-wise.text: My mother went, I went, now my kids go. Shabbat on the hill still gives me chills. The community is the product — friendships that last decades.

camp-wise.author: Teen camper
camp-wise.text: Song sessions, color war, the lake — Wise summers are the best part of my year. Bunks are old-school but nobody cares.

camp-wise.author: Parent comparing options
camp-wise.text: Wonderful ruach and caring staff. Full sessions are a real financial stretch without the scholarship help, which thankfully exists.

jcc-camp-hoover.author: Columbus parent of two
jcc-camp-hoover.text: The waterfront setting beats every strip-mall day camp in town. Bus service from the JCC helps, and Friday Shabbat sing-alongs are adorable.

jcc-camp-hoover.author: Parent of a kindergartner
jcc-camp-hoover.text: Gentle staff and great swim instruction. Drop-off traffic on the causeway can be slow, and popular weeks sell out by March.

camp-wyandot.author: Alum turned volunteer
camp-wyandot.text: The Hocking Hills setting is unbeatable and the old Camp Fire traditions survive — wood gathering, songs, ceremonies. Rustic in the truest sense.

camp-wyandot.author: Parent of a first-timer
camp-wyandot.text: Kind, scrappy little camp. Don't expect fancy facilities or constant photo updates — do expect a kid who learned to build a fire.

hiram-house-camp.author: Shaker Heights parent
hiram-house-camp.text: Real woods, real horses, twenty minutes from the city. Our daughter comes home exhausted and covered in creek water, which is exactly the point.

hiram-house-camp.author: Parent of a picky camper
hiram-house-camp.text: History and land are amazing. Bus pickup windows can run late in traffic, and rainy weeks get muddy fast. Kids don't mind; laundry does.

camp-oty-okwa.author: Parent of a scholarship camper
camp-oty-okwa.text: They made camp possible for us, full stop. My daughter hiked to waterfalls and saw the stars properly for the first time.

camp-oty-okwa.author: Former counselor
camp-oty-okwa.text: Mission-driven place with breathtaking land. Facilities are humble and some campers arrive with heavy stories, but the staff training is serious.

camp-christopher.author: Parent from a parish family
camp-christopher.text: Generations of our family have gone. Faith is present but gentle, and the lagoon and hilltop chapel are unchanged in the best way.

camp-christopher.author: Alum from the 2000s
camp-christopher.text: Wonderful traditions and dirt-cheap compared to private camps. Cabins and dining hall could use updating, but the spirit carries it.

camp-roosevelt-firebird.author: Parent of a sensitive kid
camp-roosevelt-firebird.text: They let campers choose their days, and our anxious son thrived with that freedom. Directors answer emails personally, even mid-session.

camp-roosevelt-firebird.author: Returning camper
camp-roosevelt-firebird.text: Sailing and the century-old lodges are cool. It's still growing back, so some activities are small, but that means you actually know everyone.

camp-akita.author: Columbus parent and Akita alum
camp-akita.text: Akita is a rite of passage for Columbus kids. Inclusive, joyful faith — never preachy — and counselors kids idolize. Weeks sell out in hours.

camp-akita.author: High school camper
camp-akita.text: Vespers on the point at sunset is something I think about all year. Best week of my summer, every summer.

camp-akita.author: First-time parent
camp-akita.text: Wonderful culture. Registration is genuinely stressful — set an alarm the minute it opens or you're waitlisted.

camp-wanake.author: Methodist parent
camp-wanake.text: Kids sleep in treehouses and covered wagons and help with the animals. Gentle faith formation without pressure. A hidden gem.

camp-wanake.author: Former camper
camp-wanake.text: Loved the barn and creek hikes as a kid. It's rustic and low-budget — pack bug spray and low expectations for the food.

marmon-valley-farm.author: Parent of a horse-obsessed daughter
marmon-valley-farm.text: Real horsemanship — grooming, tack, trail rides through the hills — not a trail-string photo op. Strong wholesome values throughout.

marmon-valley-farm.author: Repeat camper
marmon-valley-farm.text: You get your own horse for the week and mine remembered me from last year. The barn dance is the best night of summer.

marmon-valley-farm.author: Secular family parent
marmon-valley-farm.text: Superb riding program. Faith content is woven through daily life, so know that going in. Farm smells included free of charge.

recreation-unlimited.author: Parent of a wheelchair user
recreation-unlimited.text: Every inch of the place is accessible, so our daughter did everything — fishing, swimming, the works. Respite for us, independence for her.

recreation-unlimited.author: Longtime camp family
recreation-unlimited.text: Staff are trained, kind, and consistent year to year. Weeks fill quickly because families never leave the rotation.

scioto-hills-camp.author: Church youth leader
scioto-hills-camp.text: For a small camp the activities punch way above their weight — the blob and creek hikes are legendary with our group. Staff shepherd kids well.

scioto-hills-camp.author: Parent
scioto-hills-camp.text: Great value and our son loved paintball week. It's remote — spotty cell service for staff updates, which tested my nerves.

four-h-camp-ohio.author: 4-H parent and former camper
four-h-camp-ohio.text: Same lodge, same songs, same magic as when I went in the 80s. Teen counselors from the counties make younger kids feel famous.

four-h-camp-ohio.author: First-year camper
four-h-camp-ohio.text: Loved archery and the dances. Cabins are big and loud and the week goes by too fast.

camp-friedenswald.author: Mennonite parent
camp-friedenswald.text: Peace-church values lived out — kind counselors, four-part harmony after meals, and real attention to creation care. Our kids feel safe and known.

camp-friedenswald.author: Camper
camp-friedenswald.text: The lake and the boardwalk through the fen are beautiful. Quieter than sports camps — which I like, but high-energy kids might not.

camp-lakota.author: Parent of two campers
camp-lakota.text: The activity list is endless and the beach waterfront is a standout. Directors are hands-on and responsive.

camp-lakota.author: Camper, age 13
camp-lakota.text: Go-karts, the skate park, and Olympics week — never bored for a second.

camp-lakota.author: Alum
camp-lakota.text: Great spirit, though it's a big camp and quieter kids can take a little while to find their footing.

iroquois-springs.author: Parent of a first-time camper
iroquois-springs.text: Communication was superb and our daughter adjusted within days. Facilities look like a small college campus.

iroquois-springs.author: Alum counselor
iroquois-springs.text: Genuinely kind culture from the top down. Food gets repetitive by week six, the kids will tell you.

camp-kennybrook.author: Parent and former camper
camp-kennybrook.text: I went, my kids go, and the owners still greet us by name on visiting day. The continuity is the magic.

camp-kennybrook.author: Camper, age 14
camp-kennybrook.text: Sing and tribals are the best weeks of my year. Wi-fi-free is rough for a day, then you forget phones exist.

camp-chipinaw.author: Parent of a returning camper
camp-chipinaw.text: Strong sports coaching and the horseback program sealed it for our daughter. Traditions run deep here.

camp-chipinaw.author: Alum
camp-chipinaw.text: Chipinaw spirit is real — Sing still gives me chills. Bunks were old when I attended, though they keep renovating.

camp-lokanda.author: Parent
camp-lokanda.text: Feels like camp did in the 80s, in the best way — the owners are on the ball fields every day.

camp-lokanda.author: Camper, age 12
camp-lokanda.text: Lake toys and carnival night are awesome. Some bunks are newer than others, hope you get a new one.

camp-echo-ny.author: Parent of two campers
camp-echo-ny.text: Everything is first class, from the pools to the theater. Waitlists are real — we enrolled the sibling at age five.

camp-echo-ny.author: Alum
camp-echo-ny.text: Amazing summers and lifelong friends. It's competitive and social; laid-back kids should visit first.

timber-lake-west.author: Parent of a first-time camper
timber-lake-west.text: Four weeks was the perfect on-ramp and the staff communication was constant. Our son is begging for the full summer now.

timber-lake-west.author: Camper, age 11
timber-lake-west.text: Best counselors ever and the lake has a huge waterslide. Carnival was my favorite day.

camp-hilltop.author: Parent
camp-hilltop.text: Our kids bunked with campers from three continents and loved the horses. Family ownership shows in the details.

camp-hilltop.author: Alum
camp-hilltop.text: Friendly, mid-sized, unflashy — the kind of camp where the director flips pancakes. Facilities are tidy but not luxe.

camp-eagle-hill.author: Parent of a camper with an IEP
camp-eagle-hill.text: Our son is included in everything with just the right support. Rare to find that without a special-needs-only setting.

camp-eagle-hill.author: Parent
camp-eagle-hill.text: Kind, low-pressure environment. Program is broad rather than elite in any one activity.

frost-valley-ymca.author: Parent of three campers
frost-valley-ymca.text: Something for each of our very different kids, and financial aid that actually made it work. The valley is breathtaking.

frost-valley-ymca.author: Former counselor
frost-valley-ymca.text: Wonderful mission and inclusive culture. It's a big operation, so expect some big-camp logistics at drop-off.

camp-tel-yehudah.author: Parent of a teen
camp-tel-yehudah.text: TY gave our daughter a Jewish identity she owns, plus friends in a dozen states. The teen-only culture is the secret sauce.

camp-tel-yehudah.author: Alum
camp-tel-yehudah.text: The river, the ruach, the arguments about Israel at midnight — TY teaches you to think. Facilities are honest camp, not resort.

camp-shomria.author: Parent and movement alum
camp-shomria.text: Kids genuinely run parts of the camp and come home speaking up about values. Rustic facilities, rich culture.

camp-shomria.author: Alum
camp-shomria.text: The youth-leadership model is unlike anywhere else. Don't expect polished amenities — that's not the point.

camp-hasc.author: Parent of a camper with Down syndrome
camp-hasc.text: One-on-one staffing, therapies woven into fun, and a phone call every week with real detail. HASC is family.

camp-hasc.author: Former counselor
camp-hasc.text: The hardest and most meaningful summer of my life. The concert and the last-night energy are indescribable.

camp-agudah.author: Parent
camp-agudah.text: Serious learning in the morning, serious baseball in the afternoon. The ruach on Shabbos is unmatched.

camp-agudah.author: Alum
camp-agudah.text: A rite of passage. Bunks are basic and the canteen line is chaos, but the memories last a lifetime.

camp-bnos.author: Mother of two campers
camp-bnos.text: I went to Bnos, my girls go to Bnos. The plays and the singing are professional-level and the friendships are forever.

camp-bnos.author: Alum
camp-bnos.text: The color war breakouts are legendary. Facilities are simple — you go for the spirit, not the amenities.

camp-mogen-avraham.author: Parent
camp-mogen-avraham.text: The rebbeim and counselors genuinely bond with the boys. Our son grew in learning and in confidence.

camp-mogen-avraham.author: Alum
camp-mogen-avraham.text: Great leagues, great trips, classic Catskills bunkhouses. The last Shabbos brings grown men to tears.

camp-sternberg.author: Mother of a returning camper
camp-sternberg.text: The riding program is a huge draw and the ruach is nonstop. My daughter counts down all year.

camp-sternberg.author: Alum
camp-sternberg.text: So much energy and sisterhood. It's a big camp — girls who need quiet may find it overwhelming at first.

camp-kaylie.author: Parent of a typical camper
camp-kaylie.text: My son learned more about kindness in four weeks than in years of school — and had the best summer of his life doing it.

camp-kaylie.author: Parent of a camper with special needs
camp-kaylie.text: True inclusion, not a side program. Our daughter is simply one of the girls in her bunk.

thezone-camp.author: Parent
thezone-camp.text: The scholarship pricing opened the door and the warmth kept us. My son came home proud of his Judaism.

thezone-camp.author: Camper, age 13
thezone-camp.text: Insane activities and the staff hype is real. Davening takes getting used to if you're not religious, but nobody pressures you.

camp-emunah.author: Mother of campers
camp-emunah.text: The counselors pour their hearts into these girls. My daughters come home singing and glowing.

camp-emunah.author: Alum
camp-emunah.text: Rich in spirit and tradition; the grounds are older Catskills stock but nobody's looking at the buildings.

camp-monroe.author: Parent
camp-monroe.text: Close to the city, warm Jewish feel without being religious, and the same family running it for decades.

camp-monroe.author: Alum
camp-monroe.text: Friday night services by the lake are the memory that sticks. Mid-sized camp, big heart.

camp-nubar.author: Armenian-American parent
camp-nubar.text: Our kids found their culture and their best friends in the same summer. Affordable for what it gives.

camp-nubar.author: Alum
camp-nubar.text: Nubar is where diaspora kids become lifelong family. The kef nights and lake days live rent-free in my head.

dec-camp-debruce.author: Parent
dec-camp-debruce.text: My daughter cast her first fly rod on the Willowemoc and came home a conservationist. Registration sells out almost instantly.

dec-camp-debruce.author: Former camper
dec-camp-debruce.text: Hands-on ecology all day with counselors who actually work in the field. One week is never enough.

camp-redwood-ny.author: Parent
camp-redwood-ny.text: Close enough for easy visiting day, and the owners treat campers like their own kids.

camp-redwood-ny.author: Camper, age 12
camp-redwood-ny.text: Loved the pool and gaga. Some activities get crowded on rainy days.

total-tennis.author: Parent of a tournament player
total-tennis.text: Her strokes were visibly rebuilt in two weeks and she loved the coaches. Serious reps without boot-camp misery.

total-tennis.author: Camper, age 14
total-tennis.text: Tons of court time and good drills. If you don't love tennis, this is way too much tennis.

ymca-camp-greenkill.author: Parent from Queens
ymca-camp-greenkill.text: Great value and diverse bunks that look like our city. Some counselors were greener than others.

ymca-camp-greenkill.author: Alum
ymca-camp-greenkill.text: My first time seeing stars without streetlights. Y camps like this change city kids' lives.

tranquillity-camp.author: Parent
tranquillity-camp.text: Sleepaway camp at a price that actually works for a normal family, with traditions going back a hundred years.

tranquillity-camp.author: Alum donor
tranquillity-camp.text: Tranquillity raised generations of us. Simple bunks, big feelings, Friday night candles by the pool.

camp-tecumseh-ymca.author: Indianapolis parent of three
camp-tecumseh-ymca.text: The counselors are the secret — college kids who treat it like a calling. Our children come home kinder. Registration sells out in minutes, plan ahead.

camp-tecumseh-ymca.author: Camper, fifth summer
camp-tecumseh-ymca.text: Chapel by the river, the trading post, river day — Tecumseh is my favorite place on earth.

camp-tecumseh-ymca.author: Parent new to Indiana
camp-tecumseh-ymca.text: Lives up to the hype, though the Christian emphasis is stronger than at most Y camps and spots for new families are scarce.

ymca-camp-crosley.author: Fort Wayne parent
ymca-camp-crosley.text: Lake Webster is a fantastic waterfront — real boats, real skiing. Solid value compared to fancier camps, and the staff remember returning kids.

ymca-camp-crosley.author: Camper
ymca-camp-crosley.text: Banana boat rides and campouts on the island are the best. Some cabins are new, some are ancient — pray for a new one.

ymca-camp-potawotami.author: Fort Wayne mom
ymca-camp-potawotami.text: Small enough that the director knew my son's name by Tuesday. Sweet first sleepaway experience without the big-camp chaos.

ymca-camp-potawotami.author: Former counselor
ymca-camp-potawotami.text: Tight staff community and lovely lake. Budget is modest, so don't expect flashy facilities — expect songs, s'mores, and sunburns.

ymca-camp-carson.author: Evansville parent
ymca-camp-carson.text: Best-run camp in the tri-state — thoughtful cabin placements, real communication, and kids on wakeboards by Wednesday.

ymca-camp-carson.author: Camper, age 12
ymca-camp-carson.text: The lake toys and horse trails are awesome, and the counselors act like your big siblings.

ymca-camp-carson.author: Parent
ymca-camp-carson.text: Excellent program; July humidity in southern Indiana is brutal and the cabins aren't air conditioned. Kids adapt faster than parents.

jameson-camp.author: Westside Indy parent
jameson-camp.text: Ten minutes from downtown but feels deep-woods. Staff are trained for kids with big feelings, and scholarships kept it affordable for us.

jameson-camp.author: Former camper
jameson-camp.text: Small and personal — everyone knows everyone by day two. Fewer flashy activities than big camps, more actual mentoring.

flat-rock-river-ymca-camp.author: Greenwood parent
flat-rock-river-ymca-camp.text: Our kids' first sleepaway and the staff nailed it — daily photos, gentle homesickness handling, and a muddy river hike they still talk about.

flat-rock-river-ymca-camp.author: Camper
flat-rock-river-ymca-camp.text: River tubing and horseback were great. The dining hall gets crazy loud and the newer kids' cabins fill up first.

camp-riley-bradford-woods-in.author: Parent of a Riley Hospital patient
camp-riley-bradford-woods-in.text: Medical staff on site, zero barriers, and my daughter ziplined from her chair. We cried at pickup — happy tears.

camp-riley-bradford-woods-in.author: Volunteer counselor
camp-riley-bradford-woods-in.text: The adapted high ropes and accessible treehouse are world-class. The waitlist is the only heartbreak.

happy-hollow-camp.author: Indianapolis parent
happy-hollow-camp.text: Sliding-scale pricing meant both our kids could go. It's simple, sincere camping — hikes, campfires, and counselors who care.

happy-hollow-camp.author: Alum
happy-hollow-camp.text: The hollow itself is gorgeous. Facilities are dated and the hills are no joke, but that place raised half of south Indy.

camp-livingston.author: Cincinnati Jewish parent
camp-livingston.text: Smaller and warmer than the big-name Jewish camps up north. Shabbat by the lake is beautiful and the staff shlichim bring Israel to life.

camp-livingston.author: Camper, third summer
camp-livingston.text: Maccabiah week is everything. My whole friend group is camp friends now.

camp-livingston.author: Louisville parent
camp-livingston.text: Lovely community feel. Facilities are rustic and enrollment is small, so activity variety trails the giant camps — but nobody gets lost in the crowd.

goldman-union-camp-institute.author: Parent and GUCI alum
goldman-union-camp-institute.text: The song-session culture is legendary — my kids came home harmonizing the same melodies I learned in the 90s. Reform Judaism at its joyful best.

goldman-union-camp-institute.author: Chicago-area parent
goldman-union-camp-institute.text: Staff communication and camper care were superb. Worth the drive past the Wisconsin options.

goldman-union-camp-institute.author: Teen camper
goldman-union-camp-institute.text: Avodah and Shabbat are the highlights. The Indiana heat and flat campus aren't glamorous, but the ruach makes up for it.

camp-alexander-mack.author: Brethren parent
camp-alexander-mack.text: Simple living, service, and lake swims — Camp Mack embodies our tradition. The Becker Lodge meals alone are worth the week.

camp-alexander-mack.author: Camper
camp-alexander-mack.text: Canoeing and campfires are great, and everyone's really kind. Quieter than my friends' camps, which suits me fine.

epworth-forest-camp.author: Methodist youth pastor
epworth-forest-camp.text: Epworth summers shaped half the pastors in our conference. Great worship band culture and a classic lakefront.

epworth-forest-camp.author: Parent
epworth-forest-camp.text: My daughter loved the lake and evening celebrations. Lodging ranges from nice lodges to well-worn cabins — luck of the draw.

camp-illiana.author: Parent from a small-town church
camp-illiana.text: Half our congregation grew up at Illiana. Affordable, sincere, and the staff treat every kid like their own.

camp-illiana.author: Teen camper
camp-illiana.text: The lake blob and 9-square are highlights. Cabins get sweaty in July, and you will sing — a lot.

pyoca-camp-and-retreat-center.author: Presbyterian parent
pyoca-camp-and-retreat-center.text: Pyoca is small on purpose — my kids are known by name and welcomed exactly as they are. Progressive, gracious faith community.

pyoca-camp-and-retreat-center.author: Camper
pyoca-camp-and-retreat-center.text: The lake and creek hikes are peaceful, and vespers is my favorite. Wish there were a few more activity choices.

camp-millhouse.author: Parent of an adult camper
camp-millhouse.text: Millhouse has welcomed our son for fifteen summers. The staff-to-camper ratio and pure joy at this place are unmatched in the region.

camp-millhouse.author: Volunteer
camp-millhouse.text: Talent show night will restore your faith in humanity. Small campus, enormous heart.

ymca-camp-duncan.author: Chicago parent
ymca-camp-duncan.text: An hour from the city and well-oiled after 100 years. Financial aid made two weeks possible, and our daughter learned to sail.

ymca-camp-duncan.author: Camper
ymca-camp-duncan.text: The lake trampoline and all-camp games are great. Dining hall food is fine, cabins are cozy-old.

jcys-camp-henry-horner.author: North Shore parent
jcys-camp-henry-horner.text: Close enough for nervous first-timers, and the staff are nurturing pros. Friday night traditions are sweet without being heavy.

jcys-camp-henry-horner.author: Alum
jcys-camp-henry-horner.text: Horner summers were formative. It's smaller than the Wisconsin giants, which meant everyone got a part in the play.

ymca-camp-benson.author: Rockford parent
ymca-camp-benson.text: The driftless-area scenery surprises everyone — real bluffs in Illinois. Small camp where counselors actually mentor.

ymca-camp-benson.author: Camper
ymca-camp-benson.text: Loved the creek and climbing tower. Cabins are rustic and you'll meet some daddy longlegs.

little-grassy-united-methodist-camp.author: Downstate Methodist parent
little-grassy-united-methodist-camp.text: Gorgeous lake setting and counselors from our own churches. Simple, affordable, grounding.

little-grassy-united-methodist-camp.author: Camper
little-grassy-united-methodist-camp.text: Fishing and canoeing were my favorites. Not many fancy activities, but campfire nights are great.

illinois-4-h-memorial-camp.author: 4-H family parent
illinois-4-h-memorial-camp.text: Affordable, wholesome, and the college-student staff from U of I are wonderful role models. The Allerton woods are a bonus.

illinois-4-h-memorial-camp.author: First-time camper
illinois-4-h-memorial-camp.text: The pool and boats were fun and I made friends fast. Central Illinois in July is hot — the fans work hard.

east-bay-camp.author: Bloomington-Normal parent
east-bay-camp.text: Twenty minutes from town and steeped in local history — half the adults I know went here. Kind staff, fair price.

east-bay-camp.author: Alum
east-bay-camp.text: Great lake days and chapel by the water. Some buildings show their decades, but the bones and the spirit are good.

lutheran-outdoor-ministries-center.author: Lutheran parent
lutheran-outdoor-ministries-center.text: Our synod's kids grow up here. Counselors lead honest faith conversations and the prairie-and-woods land is lovely.

lutheran-outdoor-ministries-center.author: Camper
lutheran-outdoor-ministries-center.text: Sleeping in the covered wagons was the coolest. Food was just okay, campfire worship was the best part.

timber-pointe-outdoor-center.author: Parent of a camper with cerebral palsy
timber-pointe-outdoor-center.text: Pontoon rides, adapted fishing, nurses on site — and a kid who came home talking about friends, not barriers.

timber-pointe-outdoor-center.author: Repeat camp family
timber-pointe-outdoor-center.text: Staff training is visibly excellent. Sessions are short and demand is high, so register the day it opens.

camp-tuckabatchee.author: Ottawa-area parent
camp-tuckabatchee.text: Everybody's grandma went to Tuck. It's inexpensive, unplugged, and run with love by people who grew up there.

camp-tuckabatchee.author: Camper
camp-tuckabatchee.text: The creek, the council ring, the night hikes — I never want to leave on Saturday.

camp-tuckabatchee.author: Parent
camp-tuckabatchee.text: Charmingly old-fashioned, which also means aging cabins and simple food. The traditions carry it.

camp-kupugani.author: Chicago parent of a biracial camper
camp-kupugani.text: The only camp we found where diversity is the design, not a brochure photo. My daughter came home with real conflict-resolution language.

camp-kupugani.author: Camper, age 12
camp-kupugani.text: Small enough that nobody's invisible. The empowerment workshops sound cheesy but they actually stick with you.

camp-kupugani.author: Parent
camp-kupugani.text: Wonderful intentional community; note it's pricier than area Y camps and the camper cohort is small by design.

camp-shaw-waw-nas-see.author: Kankakee County parent
camp-shaw-waw-nas-see.text: Close to home, easy on the wallet, and my son caught his first fish in Rock Creek. The limestone canyon hikes are a surprise.

camp-shaw-waw-nas-see.author: Camper
camp-shaw-waw-nas-see.text: Creek walks and gaga ball all day. Cabins are old but the counselors make it fun.

camp-manitoqua.author: Frankfort parent
camp-manitoqua.text: A real-woods day camp in the suburbs — climbing tower, zipline, mud. Faith content is warm, not pushy. Great counselors year after year.

camp-manitoqua.author: Parent of two
camp-manitoqua.text: Kids love theme weeks. Aftercare hours are limited compared to corporate camps, which complicates work schedules.

banner-day-camp.author: Highland Park parent
banner-day-camp.text: Immaculate facilities, incredible swim instruction, and buses that arrive to the minute. It's the country club of day camps.

banner-day-camp.author: Parent of a 4-year-old
banner-day-camp.text: They handle little ones brilliantly — sunscreen reapplied, photos daily, gentle counselors. Worth every penny, and it is many pennies.

banner-day-camp.author: Value-minded parent
banner-day-camp.text: Kids have a blast, no question. The tuition and the add-ons are eye-watering, and full-summer families get priority.

decoma-day-camp.author: Northbrook parent
decoma-day-camp.text: Same owners greet you at the gate, and half the counselors were once campers. Warmer and slightly saner-priced than the flashiest local camps.

decoma-day-camp.author: Parent of a swimmer
decoma-day-camp.text: Excellent swim program and easy busing. Popular weeks close out early and the waitlist is real.

apachi-day-camp-northbrook.author: Jewish parent in the suburbs
apachi-day-camp-northbrook.text: Shabbat sing-alongs, Israeli counselors, and a genuinely joyful vibe. Inclusion aides for our son were seamless.

apachi-day-camp-northbrook.author: Interfaith family parent
apachi-day-camp-northbrook.text: Welcoming to everyone. Buses run a bit long from the far suburbs, and popular sessions fill by winter.

camp-greene-wood.author: DuPage troop parent
camp-greene-wood.text: Girl-led everything, and my daughter came home smelling like campfire and confidence. Affordable compared to private day camps.

camp-greene-wood.author: Camper
camp-greene-wood.text: Archery and the creek are the best. Some days feel long when it's 95 degrees out.

chicago-park-district-day-camp.author: Two-camper city parent
chicago-park-district-day-camp.text: Six weeks of camp for less than one week of private camp. Field trips, pool days, and counselors from the neighborhood. Registration night is the Hunger Games.

chicago-park-district-day-camp.author: Northside parent
chicago-park-district-day-camp.text: Quality varies by park — ours has fantastic veteran staff, a friend's was disorganized. Tour your park first.

chicago-park-district-day-camp.author: Former camper turned counselor
chicago-park-district-day-camp.text: Park camp raised me. It's not fancy, but the city kids get real summer: sprinklers, kickball, and the bus to the beach.

camp-scatico.author: Parent and Scatico alum
camp-scatico.text: The same head staff for decades, and the same values I grew up with. My kids' friendships mirror mine from the 90s.

camp-scatico.author: Camper, age 15
camp-scatico.text: Line-ups, league games, and the last-night circle — Scatico doesn't need waterslides to be perfect.

camp-scatico.author: Alum
camp-scatico.text: Deliberately unfancy — bunks are basic and canteen is modest. That's part of the charm, but know it going in.

camp-pontiac.author: Parent of two campers
camp-pontiac.text: Facilities rival a country club and the athletics program is genuinely strong. Enrollment is competitive — get on lists early.

camp-pontiac.author: Alum
camp-pontiac.text: Unforgettable summers, but it's a sporty, social pressure-cooker at times. Thrives for outgoing kids.

camp-kinder-ring.author: Third-generation KR parent
camp-kinder-ring.text: My grandmother sang the same Yiddish songs on the same lake. Warm, values-driven, and reasonably priced.

camp-kinder-ring.author: Alum
camp-kinder-ring.text: Culture night and the lake are highlights. Facilities are serviceable rather than shiny.

camp-sprout-lake.author: Parent of a first-time camper
camp-sprout-lake.text: Perfect starter camp — small enough to feel held, spirited enough to feel like real camp. The Israeli staff are wonderful.

camp-sprout-lake.author: Alum
camp-sprout-lake.text: Sprout Lake to Tel Yehudah is a whole Jewish childhood pipeline, and it works. Shabbat shira still lives in my head.

surprise-lake-camp.author: Parent
surprise-lake-camp.text: Financial aid made camp possible for us, and nobody can tell who's on scholarship — that ethos runs deep here.

surprise-lake-camp.author: Alum
surprise-lake-camp.text: A hundred and twenty years of NYC kids finding the woods. Rustic, warm, zero snobbery.

surprise-lake-camp.author: Camper, age 12
surprise-lake-camp.text: The lake and the farm animals are great. Bunks are old and buggy sometimes, honestly.

eden-village-camp.author: Parent
eden-village-camp.text: Our kid came home making sourdough and singing niggunim. The kindness culture is real — zero tolerance for cliques.

eden-village-camp.author: Alum counselor
eden-village-camp.text: Farm-to-table meals kids grew themselves. It's granola in the best way; sports kids may miss leagues.

camp-rising-sun.author: Alum from abroad
camp-rising-sun.text: Sixty teens from thirty countries governing themselves — it rewired my sense of what's possible. And it's completely free.

camp-rising-sun.author: Parent of a selected camper
camp-rising-sun.text: The application was rigorous but the experience was transformative. Spartan living, world-class conversations.

camp-herrlich.author: Parent
camp-herrlich.text: Scholarships, kind staff, and kids from every background sharing a bunk. Old camp soul an hour from the city.

camp-herrlich.author: Alum
camp-herrlich.text: Small lake, big heart. Program is simple — swimming, crafts, campfires — and that was plenty.

camp-felix.author: Foster parent
camp-felix.text: The counselors understood our kids' histories without ever making them feel different. They came home lighter.

camp-felix.author: Volunteer
camp-felix.text: Kids who've had the hardest years get the biggest welcome. A deeply intentional place.

camp-mariah.author: Parent from the Bronx
camp-mariah.text: Free, safe, and genuinely enriching — my daughter came home talking about careers she'd never heard of.

camp-mariah.author: Alum
camp-mariah.text: Camp Mariah showed me a bigger world. The counselors pushed us to dream concretely, not vaguely.

camp-tommy.author: Parent
camp-tommy.text: Strong male mentors and two weeks in real woods — exactly what my son needed at fourteen.

camp-tommy.author: Alum
camp-tommy.text: The hikes and the brotherhood stuck with me. Cabins are simple and the days are full.

camp-anita-bliss-coler.author: Parent from Brooklyn
camp-anita-bliss-coler.text: My daughter's first time away from the block, and she blossomed. The Fund handles everything with such care.

camp-anita-bliss-coler.author: Alum
camp-anita-bliss-coler.text: ABC gave me the woods, a pool, and friends from every borough. Free never felt so rich.

camp-olmsted.author: Grandparent
camp-olmsted.text: Three generations of our family went up the Hudson to Olmsted. It remains humble, safe, and loving.

camp-olmsted.author: Former counselor
camp-olmsted.text: A mission with a waterfront. Facilities are dated but the purpose is timeless.

camp-ma-he-tu.author: Parent
camp-ma-he-tu.text: A hidden gem — real sleepaway camp at day-camp prices, with a lake all their own inside the state park.

camp-ma-he-tu.author: Alum
camp-ma-he-tu.text: Ma-He-Tu girls come back as counselors, always. Simple cabins, huge sisterhood.

camp-foster-ymca.author: Des Moines parent and alum
camp-foster-ymca.text: Okoboji is Iowa's best lake and Foster owns the best stretch of it. The songs and traditions haven't changed in decades, thank goodness.

camp-foster-ymca.author: Camper, age 11
camp-foster-ymca.text: The water trampoline and polar bear swims are legendary. I cried when my week ended.

camp-foster-ymca.author: Parent
camp-foster-ymca.text: Well-run and spirited. Cabins are genuinely rustic and July weeks sell out months out.

des-moines-y-camp.author: Ankeny parent
