des-moines-y-camp.text: The river valley setting is stunning by Iowa standards. Counselor culture is strong — our son wants to work there someday.

des-moines-y-camp.author: Former camper
des-moines-y-camp.text: Chapel on the bluff and canoe trips made my summers. Mosquitoes by the river demand respect.

camp-hantesa.author: Camp Fire parent
camp-hantesa.text: Hantesa keeps the old Camp Fire ceremonies alive and every kid belongs. The riverside cabins and horse barn are well-loved.

camp-hantesa.author: Camper
camp-hantesa.text: I love the horses and the craft lodge. Some cabins are ancient, but that's part of the story.

camp-wyoming.author: Presbyterian parent
camp-wyoming.text: Kids feed the animals, muck around the creek, and talk about faith without any pressure. Honest Iowa camping.

camp-wyoming.author: Camper
camp-wyoming.text: The mud hike is famous for a reason. Bring shoes you can throw away.

lutheran-lakeside-camp.author: Lutheran parent
lutheran-lakeside-camp.text: Beachfront church camp — hard to beat. The college counselors sing grace like a concert and mean it.

lutheran-lakeside-camp.author: Camper
lutheran-lakeside-camp.text: Swimming in Spirit Lake every day is amazing. Windy days cancel the good water stuff sometimes.

riverside-lutheran-bible-camp.author: Parent and former counselor
riverside-lutheran-bible-camp.text: Riverside staff alumni are everywhere in Iowa ministry. Strong Bible teaching, goofy skits, river campfires — the formula works.

riverside-lutheran-bible-camp.author: Camper
riverside-lutheran-bible-camp.text: Loved the river and the night games. Morning Bible study is early, like really early.

camp-courageous-of-iowa.author: Parent of a camper with Down syndrome
camp-courageous-of-iowa.text: Entirely donor-funded, which still amazes me. Our daughter canoes, rides the zipline, and counts down the days all year.

camp-courageous-of-iowa.author: Respite-week parent
camp-courageous-of-iowa.text: The only week of the year we truly rest, because we trust them completely. Staff are extraordinary.

camp-io-dis-e-ca.author: LCMS parent
camp-io-dis-e-ca.text: Doctrinally solid and personally warm — a rare combination. My kids love the lake days and the pastor-led campfires.

camp-io-dis-e-ca.author: Camper
camp-io-dis-e-ca.text: Fun week with good friends. The cabins are simple and the schedule is pretty set, but the counselors are great.

iowa-4-h-center.author: County extension parent
iowa-4-h-center.text: Hands-on learning disguised as fun — my kids came home quoting facts about prairie restoration. Great value.

iowa-4-h-center.author: Camper
iowa-4-h-center.text: The ropes course and river hikes were awesome. Lodge bunkrooms are big and loud at night.

camp-sunnyside.author: Parent of twins, one with a disability
camp-sunnyside.text: Both my kids attend together — that's the magic. Adaptive bikes, patient staff, and real friendships across abilities.

camp-sunnyside.author: Parent
camp-sunnyside.text: Wonderful mission and staff. Sessions fill early and the daily schedule leans structured.

camp-abe-lincoln.author: Davenport parent
camp-abe-lincoln.text: Close to home but feels far away. Financial aid is generous and the staff called mid-week just to say our son was thriving.

camp-abe-lincoln.author: Camper
camp-abe-lincoln.text: The creek hikes and campouts are the best. Pool time beats the pond, honestly.

ymca-camp-wapsie.author: Cedar Rapids parent
ymca-camp-wapsie.text: Wapsie is a family religion here — grandparents, parents, kids. The Sunday closing circle gets everyone misty.

ymca-camp-wapsie.author: Camper, fourth year
ymca-camp-wapsie.text: River canoeing and the horse trails are my favorite. The camp songs live in my head all year.

ymca-camp-wapsie.author: Parent
ymca-camp-wapsie.text: Beloved for good reason, though popular weeks are wait-listed by February. Cabins are old-school wood and screen.

camp-wood-ymca.author: Wichita parent
camp-wood-ymca.text: The Flint Hills at dusk from horseback — city kids don't forget that. Staff culture is warm and the history runs a century deep.

camp-wood-ymca.author: Alum
camp-wood-ymca.text: Camp Wood made me love Kansas. The prairie, the lake, the songs in Nicholson Lodge.

camp-wood-ymca.author: Parent
camp-wood-ymca.text: Wonderful traditions; pack for real heat and wind. It's remote, which is the point and also the logistics challenge.

rock-springs-ranch-4-h-camp.author: 4-H parent
rock-springs-ranch-4-h-camp.text: The spring keeps the swimming area cold even in a Kansas July. Well-staffed, well-fed, and full of tradition.

rock-springs-ranch-4-h-camp.author: Camper
rock-springs-ranch-4-h-camp.text: Horses and riflery were my favorites. The hills make you strong and the cicadas are LOUD.

camp-chippewa-ks.author: Methodist parent
camp-chippewa-ks.text: Small, sincere, and our conference's kids have gone for generations. The lakefront chapel is special.

camp-chippewa-ks.author: Camper
camp-chippewa-ks.text: Canoes and campfires were great. Not a ton of activities, but I liked the slower pace.

white-memorial-camp.author: Disciples parent
white-memorial-camp.text: Surrounded by lake on a prairie peninsula — the setting does half the ministry. Humble facilities, huge hearts.

white-memorial-camp.author: Alum
white-memorial-camp.text: My favorite childhood weeks. It's small and simple; wind off the lake keeps the heat honest.

camp-mennoscah.author: Mennonite parent
camp-mennoscah.text: Floating the Ninnescah on inner tubes is a rite of passage. Simple living, deep community, absurdly reasonable price.

camp-mennoscah.author: Camper
camp-mennoscah.text: River time is the best. Kansas sun is no joke and the cabins are basic, but I love this place.

ymca-camp-hyde.author: Wichita working parent
ymca-camp-hyde.text: Affordable weeks all summer, buses from our local Y, and my kids come home covered in creek mud and stories.

ymca-camp-hyde.author: Parent
ymca-camp-hyde.text: Great classic camp days — archery, fishing, canoes. Communication is basic and hot weeks are hot.

ymca-camp-kitaki.author: Lincoln parent
ymca-camp-kitaki.text: Kitaki counselors are rock stars to my kids. The Platte River setting and century of traditions make it Nebraska's gold standard.

ymca-camp-kitaki.author: Camper, age 12
ymca-camp-kitaki.text: The mud pit, the horse trails, campout night on the river — best week of my whole year.

ymca-camp-kitaki.author: Parent
ymca-camp-kitaki.text: Excellent program, though sessions sell out fast and summer storms sometimes scramble the schedule. Staff handle it well.

camp-calvin-crest.author: Presbyterian parent
camp-calvin-crest.text: The bluff trails surprise people who think Nebraska is flat. Gentle, thoughtful staff and a real sense of sanctuary.

camp-calvin-crest.author: Alum
camp-calvin-crest.text: Vespers overlooking the Platte valley shaped my faith. Facilities are humble; the place itself does the work.

maranatha-bible-camp.author: Western Nebraska parent
maranatha-bible-camp.text: The only real camp for hours in any direction, and they do it well — huge lake toys, solid teaching, kids come home changed.

maranatha-bible-camp.author: Teen camper
maranatha-bible-camp.text: The blob and banana boats are awesome. Chapel runs long some nights, but the speakers are usually good.

camp-comeca.author: Methodist parent
camp-comeca.text: The canyon setting is a hidden Nebraska gem. Small camp where the director knows every family by name.

camp-comeca.author: Camper
camp-comeca.text: Loved the pool and night hikes. Not the fanciest camp, but my cabin group was the best.

camp-fontanelle.author: Omaha-area parent
camp-fontanelle.text: Ziplines and treehouses at church-camp prices. Staff were wonderful with our first-timer's nerves.

camp-fontanelle.author: Camper
camp-fontanelle.text: The zipline is the best part, and the corn pit. Wish the week were longer.

timberlake-ranch-camps.author: Covenant church parent
timberlake-ranch-camps.text: Real ranch riding — my daughter learned to saddle, groom, and lope by Friday. Sweet staff, strong values.

timberlake-ranch-camps.author: Camper
timberlake-ranch-camps.text: Horses every day and river hikes. Cabins get warm, but evenings on the bluff are perfect.

nebraska-state-4-h-camp-halsey.author: Sandhills ranch parent
nebraska-state-4-h-camp-halsey.text: A forest in the middle of the Sandhills — kids never get over it. Affordable and full of 4-H tradition.

nebraska-state-4-h-camp-halsey.author: Camper
nebraska-state-4-h-camp-halsey.text: Climbing the fire tower trail was the highlight. It's remote, which makes the stars incredible.

eastern-nebraska-4-h-center.author: Omaha parent
eastern-nebraska-4-h-center.text: Twenty minutes from home and my kids fish, canoe, and shoot archery all day. University-trained staff are a plus.

eastern-nebraska-4-h-center.author: Parent
eastern-nebraska-4-h-center.text: Great value day camp. Pickup lines get slow on Fridays and popular STEM weeks fill early.

esther-k-newman-camp.author: Omaha Jewish parent
esther-k-newman-camp.text: For Jewish kids scattered across the Plains, this camp is where they finally feel like the majority. Shabbat outdoors is lovely.

esther-k-newman-camp.author: Camper
esther-k-newman-camp.text: Small camp, big friendships. Fewer activities than giant camps, but everyone gets to do everything.

camp-luther-ne.author: LCMS parent
camp-luther-ne.text: Solid Lutheran teaching and counselors who clearly love the kids. Our congregation sends a van every summer.

camp-luther-ne.author: Camper
camp-luther-ne.text: Gaga ball, the pond, and campfire skits — simple and fun. Food is church-basement classic.

international-music-camp.author: Band director and parent
international-music-camp.text: Faculty from conservatories on both sides of the border, and the final concerts in the Peace Garden are genuinely moving. Incredible value for serious young musicians.

international-music-camp.author: Student camper
international-music-camp.text: A week of nothing but music with kids who actually care about it. I improved more in seven days than a whole semester.

international-music-camp.author: Parent
international-music-camp.text: Wonderful instruction; the location is remote for most families and dorm-style housing is bare-bones. Bring a good pillow and a mute.

triangle-y-camp.author: Minot parent
triangle-y-camp.text: Sakakawea is an inland sea and the camp makes the most of it — boats, beach, sunsets. Staff feel like family.

triangle-y-camp.author: Camper
triangle-y-camp.text: Tubing behind the boat was the best. Wind days mean more field games than lake time.

red-willow-bible-camp.author: Small-town parent
red-willow-bible-camp.text: Three generations of our family are Red Willow campers. The lake, the songs, the caramel rolls — unchanged and perfect.

red-willow-bible-camp.author: Camper
red-willow-bible-camp.text: Swimming and the giant swing are great. Not much else nearby, but that's kind of the point.

camp-of-the-cross.author: Bismarck parent
camp-of-the-cross.text: The view over Sakakawea from the chapel hill is worth the drive alone. Kind staff, honest food, deep roots.

camp-of-the-cross.author: Camper
camp-of-the-cross.text: Fishing and beach days were awesome. The wind can howl, and the walleye don't always cooperate.

wesley-acres-camp.author: Methodist parent
wesley-acres-camp.text: Tiny camp, huge heart. My kids know every counselor and the price fits a farm-family budget.

wesley-acres-camp.author: Camper
wesley-acres-camp.text: Canoeing the Sheyenne and campfires were the best. Mosquitoes are a lifestyle out here.

crystal-springs-baptist-camp.author: Baptist parent
crystal-springs-baptist-camp.text: Straightforward gospel teaching and a surprisingly nice lake. Our kids count the days every June.

crystal-springs-baptist-camp.author: Camper
crystal-springs-baptist-camp.text: The waterslide into the lake is the highlight. Cabin bunks squeak like crazy, but camp friends are the best friends.

storm-mountain-center.author: Rapid City parent
storm-mountain-center.text: Real Black Hills camping — granite, ponderosa, deer through camp every evening. Gentle, welcoming staff.

storm-mountain-center.author: Camper
storm-mountain-center.text: The hikes to the overlook are amazing. It's rustic and chilly at night, even in July.

nesodak-bible-camp.author: Watertown parent
nesodak-bible-camp.text: Enemy Swim is a gorgeous, clean lake and the camp lives on it — swimming, sailing, fishing. Counselors radiate kindness.

nesodak-bible-camp.author: Camper
nesodak-bible-camp.text: Polar plunge every morning, worth it every time. Wind can cancel canoe day, sadly.

camp-judson.author: Baptist parent
camp-judson.text: Minutes from Rushmore but wonderfully hidden in the pines. Small enough that shy kids bloom.

camp-judson.author: Camper
camp-judson.text: Creek hikes and campfires under the granite are awesome. Nights get cold — pack the good sleeping bag.

placerville-camp.author: UCC parent
placerville-camp.text: Progressive, welcoming faith in a beautiful canyon. Every kid — every kid — belongs here, and they mean it.

placerville-camp.author: Alum
placerville-camp.text: Creek dips and hillside vespers made my childhood. Buildings are old; bring a flashlight and low expectations for water pressure.

byron-bible-camp.author: Huron-area parent
byron-bible-camp.text: Half our town grew up at Byron. Affordable, sincere, and the staff pray for each camper by name.

byron-bible-camp.author: Camper
byron-bible-camp.text: The river and night games are great. It's simple — that's kind of what makes it good.

swan-lake-christian-camp.author: Freeman-area parent
swan-lake-christian-camp.text: Peace-church values, a warm lake, and food that tastes like a grandma made it — because one probably did.

swan-lake-christian-camp.author: Camper
swan-lake-christian-camp.text: Tubing and the mud volleyball pit are the best. The lake gets weedy by August.

camp-leif-ericson.author: Sioux Falls parent and alum
camp-leif-ericson.text: Every Sioux Falls kid does Leif — the Viking ship, the songs, the woods. Now I watch my kids run down the same trails.

camp-leif-ericson.author: Parent of a kindergartner
camp-leif-ericson.text: Organized, affordable, and magical for little kids. Registration morning crashes the website every single year.

camp-leif-ericson.author: Parent
camp-leif-ericson.text: Fantastic classic day camp. Hot weeks mean a lot of sunscreen and the pickup line takes patience.

camp-seneca-lake.author: Parent and CSL alum
camp-seneca-lake.text: Shabbat overlooking the lake is one of the most beautiful things my kids experience all year. The community is everything.

camp-seneca-lake.author: Camper, age 13
camp-seneca-lake.text: Sailing, banana boating, and the friendships — I live for CSL summers.

camp-seneca-lake.author: Alum
camp-seneca-lake.text: Warm and inclusive with strong traditions. Cabins are basic and hills are steep — your calves will know.

ymca-camp-cory.author: Parent
ymca-camp-cory.text: The sailing fleet is impressive for a Y camp and the counselors set a wonderful tone. Great value on a gorgeous lake.

ymca-camp-cory.author: Alum
ymca-camp-cory.text: Keuka sunsets from the dock are unbeatable. Cabins get hot in July; the lake fixes that fast.

camp-whitman.author: Parent
camp-whitman.text: Inclusive, kind, and affordable. Their week for campers with special needs is run with real expertise.

camp-whitman.author: Alum
camp-whitman.text: Lake vespers and s'mores — simple church camp done right. Program is mellow by design.

long-point-camp.author: Parent
long-point-camp.text: Our church sponsored both kids for a week that cost us almost nothing. They came home full of songs and stories.

long-point-camp.author: Former counselor
long-point-camp.text: Kids who've never left their block get a Finger Lakes waterfront. Facilities are institutional but well-kept.

camp-stella-maris.author: Rochester parent
camp-stella-maris.text: Everyone we know went to CSM as a kid and now sends their own. The waterfront staff are superb.

camp-stella-maris.author: Alum
camp-stella-maris.text: The lake and the traditions carry it. Cabins are shoulder-to-shoulder bunks — part of the fun, honestly.

casowasco-camp.author: Parent
casowasco-camp.text: Gorgeous lakefront and counselors who clearly love the place. Our non-churchy kid felt completely at home.

casowasco-camp.author: Alum
casowasco-camp.text: Sunrise on Owasco from the chapel point is something I still think about decades later.

lourdes-camp.author: Syracuse parent
lourdes-camp.text: That lake is the cleanest water in the state and the staff culture is wholesome without being stiff.

lourdes-camp.author: Alum
lourdes-camp.text: Generations of CNY families in one place. Rustic bunks, legendary bug juice.

camp-barton.author: Scoutmaster
camp-barton.text: The waterfront on Cayuga is the star. Program staff were young but enthusiastic this year.

camp-barton.author: Former scout
camp-barton.text: Swimming in the lake and biking to the falls — pure Finger Lakes summer.

hidden-valley-4h-camp.author: Parent
hidden-valley-4h-camp.text: Unplugged, muddy, and joyful — what childhood summers are supposed to look like, at a fair price.

hidden-valley-4h-camp.author: Alum counselor
hidden-valley-4h-camp.text: Small and homey with staff who grew up there. Facilities are humble; spirit is not.

camp-bristol-hills.author: Parent
camp-bristol-hills.text: The counselors are local 4-H kids who came up through the ranks — the culture passes right down.

camp-bristol-hills.author: Camper, age 11
camp-bristol-hills.text: Polliwog pond, the big swing, and skit night. I want to go for two weeks next year.

camp-shankitunk.author: Parent
camp-shankitunk.text: Half our town's kids are there each summer. Affordable, safe, and staffed by people we know.

camp-shankitunk.author: Alum
camp-shankitunk.text: Creek hikes and campfire pudgy pies. Rustic as it gets — that's the charm.

camp-talooli.author: Parent
camp-talooli.text: Small enough that the director greets every camper by name by Tuesday. A gentle, welcoming place.

camp-talooli.author: Alum
camp-talooli.text: The wishing boats ceremony on the last night has made generations of kids cry happy tears.

camp-hollis.author: Local parent
camp-hollis.text: County-subsidized and beautifully run. The Lake Ontario sunsets from the hill are postcard material.

camp-hollis.author: Alum
camp-hollis.text: Judge Hollis's promise still holds — every kid gets their week. Proud to have been one of them.

camp-fiver.author: Parent from Harlem
camp-fiver.text: Fiver has walked with my son since he was eight — camp every summer and check-ins all year. It's not just camp, it's family.

camp-fiver.author: Fiver alum
camp-fiver.text: Ten summers, then they helped me apply to college. I can't overstate what this place does.

eastern-us-music-camp.author: Parent of a trumpet player
eastern-us-music-camp.text: College dorms, real practice rooms, and faculty who push kids kindly. He came home a stronger, prouder musician.

eastern-us-music-camp.author: Alum
eastern-us-music-camp.text: Ensemble placements motivated me all summer. Less woodsy-camp, more music-school-with-swimming.

rotary-sunshine-camp.author: Parent of a wheelchair user
rotary-sunshine-camp.text: Every inch is accessible, down to the treehouse. And they've never charged families a dime.

rotary-sunshine-camp.author: Volunteer nurse
rotary-sunshine-camp.text: A hundred years of Rotarians keeping a promise to these kids. The joy is overwhelming.

camp-abilities-brockport.author: Parent of a blind athlete
camp-abilities-brockport.text: One-on-one coaching and sports we were told she'd never play. She came home calling herself an athlete.

camp-abilities-brockport.author: Coach volunteer
camp-abilities-brockport.text: The model has spread worldwide for a reason. Watching first goals and first sprints never gets old.

ymca-camp-onyahsa.author: Jamestown parent
ymca-camp-onyahsa.text: 125 years of local kids on that waterfront. Scholarships meant our whole crew could go together.

ymca-camp-onyahsa.author: Alum
ymca-camp-onyahsa.text: Sailing at sunset on Chautauqua is core-memory material. Cabins are creaky and beloved.

ymca-camp-weona.author: Buffalo parent
ymca-camp-weona.text: Affordable, close to the city, and my son's counselor wrote him a postcard after camp. Class acts.

ymca-camp-weona.author: Alum
ymca-camp-weona.text: Mud hikes and the polar bear swim — WNY kids' shared heritage. Facilities are basic Y-camp standard.

ymca-camp-kenan.author: Parent
ymca-camp-kenan.text: The Lake Ontario bluff setting surprised us — feels like an ocean camp. Counselors were fantastic with our anxious kid.

ymca-camp-kenan.author: Alum
ymca-camp-kenan.text: Kenan pride runs deep in Niagara County. Windy days mean pool instead of lake, kids adapt.

camp-timbercrest.author: Scout parent
camp-timbercrest.text: The horse program books out first for a reason. My daughter came home smelling like barn and grinning.

camp-timbercrest.author: Alum
camp-timbercrest.text: Lake ceremonies and cookout skills I still use. It's real woods camping — embrace the latrines.

camp-seven-hills.author: Parent
camp-seven-hills.text: Classic scouting summers — my daughter's confidence doubles every session she attends.

camp-seven-hills.author: Alum
camp-seven-hills.text: From Brownie weekends to CIT summers, Seven Hills raised me. The traditions endure.

camp-li-lo-li.author: Parent
camp-li-lo-li.text: Solid Bible teaching, cold creek water, and counselors who invest in kids. Wonderful value.

camp-li-lo-li.author: Alum
camp-li-lo-li.text: Decades of WNY church kids call Li-Lo-Li home. The campfire testimonies stay with you.

circle-c-ranch.author: Parent of a horse lover
circle-c-ranch.text: Daily riding for every kid, not just token pony walks. The staff's faith is warm and genuine.

circle-c-ranch.author: Camper, age 12
circle-c-ranch.text: I got the same horse all week and learned to trot. Best week of my summer.

camp-turner-ny.author: Parent
camp-turner-ny.text: Being inside the state park means real hikes and real wildlife. Sweet, faith-filled staff.

camp-turner-ny.author: Alum
camp-turner-ny.text: Generations of Buffalo Catholic kids at Turner. Rustic cabins, unbeatable setting.

camp-findley.author: Parent
camp-findley.text: Small, sweet, and right on the water. The staff make every kid feel chosen.

camp-findley.author: Alum
camp-findley.text: Findley sunsets and canteen freeze pops — humble and happy memories.

dec-camp-rushford.author: Parent
dec-camp-rushford.text: A future game-warden's dream week for $350. My son is already asking about the hunter education add-on.

dec-camp-rushford.author: Former camper
dec-camp-rushford.text: Learned to filet a fish and read a topo map. Basic bunkhouses, brilliant instructors.

camp-hillard.author: Parent of two campers
camp-hillard.text: Everything runs like clockwork. My kids learned to swim here and the counselors are mostly teachers. Yes, it's pricey and the summer fills up early, but the polish is real.

camp-hillard.author: Scarsdale-area parent
camp-hillard.text: Wonderful for littles, very structured. Older kids can outgrow the routine by 11 or 12. Communication from the office is excellent.

mohawk-day-camp.author: Parent of a first-time camper
mohawk-day-camp.text: They handled my nervous 5-year-old beautifully. Buses were reliable and the camp photos posted daily kept us in the loop.

mohawk-day-camp.author: Former counselor
mohawk-day-camp.text: Great place to work and campers clearly love it. It's a big operation, so some days feel a little assembly-line, but the kids never notice.

elmwood-day-camp.author: Parent of a sensitive kid
elmwood-day-camp.text: Elmwood met our anxious son exactly where he was. Smaller than the mega-camps nearby and the directors know every child's name.

elmwood-day-camp.author: Returning family
elmwood-day-camp.text: Lovely culture and great swim program. Facilities are nice but not as glitzy as some Westchester camps, which honestly kept the vibe down to earth.

pierce-country-day-camp.author: Third-generation camp family
pierce-country-day-camp.text: I went here, my kids go here. The traditions, the spirit days, the swim program — nobody does it better on the North Shore. You pay for it, though.

pierce-country-day-camp.author: Parent of a kindergartner
pierce-country-day-camp.text: The attention to detail is unreal, down to sunscreen reapplication schedules. Worth the waitlist.

pierce-country-day-camp.author: Alum
pierce-country-day-camp.text: Magical summers, but it's a big polished machine — kids who want to wander and do their own thing may feel over-scheduled.

park-shore-country-day-camp.author: Dix Hills parent
park-shore-country-day-camp.text: My kids count down to camp all year. Tons of swim time and the extended-day option saved us as working parents.

park-shore-country-day-camp.author: Parent of a tween
park-shore-country-day-camp.text: Great energy and facilities. Groups can be large, and pickup traffic is its own adventure, but the kids come home happy and exhausted.

crestwood-country-day-camp.author: Parent of returning campers
crestwood-country-day-camp.text: The go-karts and pools are the stuff of legend in our house. Counselors are enthusiastic and the buses run on time.

crestwood-country-day-camp.author: Melville parent
crestwood-country-day-camp.text: Solid classic day camp. Lunch and swim are strong; a few activities feel dated, but the kids don't care one bit.

coleman-country-day-camp.author: South Shore parent
coleman-country-day-camp.text: The horseback riding sold my daughter; the daily emails and photos sold me. Staff training clearly shows.

coleman-country-day-camp.author: Parent of a camper with allergies
coleman-country-day-camp.text: Allergy handling at lunch was meticulous. It's expensive and sessions sell out by winter, so plan ahead.

west-hills-day-camp.author: Huntington parent
west-hills-day-camp.text: Feels like the camps we grew up with — trees, horses, pool time, sneakers full of sand. Ownership is hands-on and knows the kids.

west-hills-day-camp.author: Parent of a horse-obsessed kid
west-hills-day-camp.text: Riding program is the highlight. Facilities are charmingly worn in spots, but the staff warmth makes up for it.

kenwal-day-camp.author: Parent of three campers
kenwal-day-camp.text: All three of my kids thrived here at different ages. Great value compared to the flashier camps nearby.

kenwal-day-camp.author: Alum turned parent
kenwal-day-camp.text: Still has the same friendly, unfussy vibe I remember. Some equipment shows its age, but swim instruction remains excellent.

twin-oaks-country-day-camp.author: Freeport-area parent
twin-oaks-country-day-camp.text: Intimate and nurturing — a great first camp. The pool program turned my cautious kid into a swimmer in one summer.

twin-oaks-country-day-camp.author: Parent of a camper
twin-oaks-country-day-camp.text: Sweet, well-run camp. Fewer bells and whistles than the mega-camps, which suited our family fine.

north-shore-day-camp.author: Glen Cove parent
north-shore-day-camp.text: Terrific counselors and a swim program that actually teaches. Bus routes cover most of the North Shore.

north-shore-day-camp.author: Parent of two
north-shore-day-camp.text: Well-organized and fun, though sessions book up fast and the add-on fees for extras surprised us.

camps-r-us.author: Working parent
camps-r-us.text: Affordable, flexible scheduling, and my kids loved theme days. You can pick the weeks you need instead of committing to a full summer.

camps-r-us.author: Suffolk parent
camps-r-us.text: Great value. Facilities vary by campus since they use school sites, so tour the one near you first.

future-stars-summer-camps.author: Parent of a tennis kid
future-stars-summer-camps.text: College facilities and coaches who actually teach technique. We mix a tennis week with a STEAM week every summer.

future-stars-summer-camps.author: Westchester parent
future-stars-summer-camps.text: Quality varies a bit by specialty — sports programs are stronger than some of the academic ones. Weekly signup is super convenient.

young-peoples-day-camp.author: Queens parent
young-peoples-day-camp.text: Honest value: bus picks up at our corner, kids swim every day, price beats everything else. Don't expect fancy facilities.

young-peoples-day-camp.author: Brooklyn parent
young-peoples-day-camp.text: Counselors were caring and my son loved the trips. Communication could be better during busy weeks.

young-peoples-day-camp.author: Alum
young-peoples-day-camp.text: Every summer of my childhood. The bus songs alone were worth it.

hofstra-summer-camps.author: Nassau parent
hofstra-summer-camps.text: The specialty options are endless — my son did basketball, chess, and filmmaking in one summer. Real college facilities.

hofstra-summer-camps.author: Parent of a camper
hofstra-summer-camps.text: Well-run and flexible, though it feels more like enrichment classes than a woodsy camp. Parking at drop-off is hectic.

camp-jacobson.author: JCC member parent
camp-jacobson.text: Inclusive, warm, and organized. Their support for kids with different needs is the best we've seen at a day camp.

camp-jacobson.author: Roslyn-area parent
camp-jacobson.text: Great staff and pools. Fridays have a lovely Shabbat sing; the vibe is cultural, not religious. Spots go quickly to members.

kings-bay-y-summer-camp.author: Sheepshead Bay parent
kings-bay-y-summer-camp.text: Solid local option — kids swim at the Y pool and take good trips. It's a city camp, so expect school buildings, not meadows.

kings-bay-y-summer-camp.author: Parent of a camper
kings-bay-y-summer-camp.text: Counselors grew up in this community and it shows. My daughter felt at home from day one.

aviator-sports-summer-camp.author: Brooklyn parent
aviator-sports-summer-camp.text: Where else does a kid ice skate and rock climb in July? Facilities are the draw; my son never got bored.

aviator-sports-summer-camp.author: Parent of two campers
aviator-sports-summer-camp.text: Great variety, but it's indoors a lot and the group sizes swell in peak weeks. Convenient parking, at least.

asphalt-green-summer-camp.author: UES parent
asphalt-green-summer-camp.text: The swim program is the best in Manhattan, full stop. My kids came out water-safe and confident.

asphalt-green-summer-camp.author: Manhattan parent
asphalt-green-summer-camp.text: Excellent staff and facilities, though outdoor space is city-limited and the price climbs fast with extended day.

92ny-camp-yomi.author: Manhattan parent
92ny-camp-yomi.text: Best of both worlds — real grass and pools all day, home for dinner. Staff are nurturing pros and the bus counselors are great.

92ny-camp-yomi.author: Parent of a first-timer
92ny-camp-yomi.text: Wonderful gentle culture. The bus ride is long on traffic days, which was our only gripe.

ivy-league-day-camp.author: Smithtown parent
ivy-league-day-camp.text: Hot lunch included, doors-to-door buses, and my kids actually improved at swimming. Easy button for Suffolk families.

ivy-league-day-camp.author: Parent of a camper
ivy-league-day-camp.text: Very well organized, if a bit rigid with schedules. Younger kids especially get tons of attention.

camp-alvernia.author: Third-generation Alvernia family
camp-alvernia.text: Kids sail on the harbor and come home smelling like salt water. Tradition and warmth over frills, and the price is fair.

camp-alvernia.author: Alum
camp-alvernia.text: The waterfront made my childhood. It's gently Catholic in character but welcoming to everyone.

camp-alvernia.author: Parent of a camper
camp-alvernia.text: Beautiful setting and kind staff. Facilities are historic rather than shiny — that's part of the charm.

camp-anchor.author: Parent of a camper with special needs
camp-anchor.text: ANCHOR is the highlight of our year. One-to-one attention, beach days, and staff who return summer after summer because they love these kids.

camp-anchor.author: Longtime volunteer counselor
camp-anchor.text: The most joyful place on Long Island. Waitlists are real because nobody ever leaves the program.

friends-academy-summer-programs.author: North Shore parent
friends-academy-summer-programs.text: Gorgeous campus and thoughtful staff — the Quaker kindness ethos comes through. Weekly options kept our picky tween engaged.

friends-academy-summer-programs.author: Parent of a camper
friends-academy-summer-programs.text: Lovely program; more school-campus polish than woodsy adventure. Aftercare was a lifesaver.

hampton-country-day-camp.author: Summer resident
hampton-country-day-camp.text: Our city kids beg to go back every August. Staff ratios are excellent and the place sparkles.

hampton-country-day-camp.author: East End parent
hampton-country-day-camp.text: Fantastic camp, Hamptons pricing. Flexible weeks are great for families out east only part of the summer.

southampton-camp-and-club.author: Southampton parent
southampton-camp-and-club.text: Feels like a kids' country club in the best way. Communication is white-glove and the counselors are wonderful.

southampton-camp-and-club.author: Visiting family
southampton-camp-and-club.text: Beautiful and flexible for partial-summer stays; just brace for the invoice.

rolling-river-day-camp.author: Five Towns parent
rolling-river-day-camp.text: Kayaking on the canal, warm owners who know every kid, and epic color war. Our kids never want summer to end.

rolling-river-day-camp.author: Parent of a camper
rolling-river-day-camp.text: Excellent staff culture. The campus is compact compared to the sprawling camps, but they use every inch cleverly.

park-slope-day-camp.author: Park Slope parent
park-slope-day-camp.text: Counselors are pied pipers and Prospect Park is a pretty great campus. Weekly flexibility fits our patchwork summer.

park-slope-day-camp.author: Brooklyn parent
park-slope-day-camp.text: Fun, social, well-run. It's an urban camp — lots of walking and public spaces — so manage expectations about facilities.

mill-basin-day-camp.author: Brooklyn parent of two
mill-basin-day-camp.text: The kids come home hoarse from cheering. Trips are well-run and the bus service is genuinely door to door.

mill-basin-day-camp.author: Parent of a camper
mill-basin-day-camp.text: Lots of fun, though it's big and loud — quieter kids may need a beat to find their footing.

poly-summer-day-camp.author: Bay Ridge parent
poly-summer-day-camp.text: That campus in Brooklyn feels like a secret garden. Strong swim program and courteous, professional staff.

poly-summer-day-camp.author: Parent of a camper
poly-summer-day-camp.text: Great facilities; programming is a bit school-flavored, which suited our academic kid fine.

camp-settoga.author: UWS parent
camp-settoga.text: Brand-new facilities, thoughtful staff, and my kids get a real out-of-the-city camp day. The bus is long but they nap.

camp-settoga.author: Manhattan parent
camp-settoga.text: Beautiful campus and warm inclusive culture. Pricey, and the commute eats the day for littler kids.

corbins-crusaders-day-camp.author: NYC parent
corbins-crusaders-day-camp.text: My sports-mad son found his tribe. Real coaching, real games, and the bus from the city is painless.

corbins-crusaders-day-camp.author: Greenwich parent
corbins-crusaders-day-camp.text: Terrific for athletic kids; less so for the arts-and-crafts crowd. Facilities are functional rather than fancy.

camp-chen-a-wanda.author: Alum who did nine summers
camp-chen-a-wanda.text: CAW is the kind of place where your bunkmates end up in your wedding party. Color war is legendary. Seven weeks is a big commitment, but that's exactly why the friendships stick.

camp-chen-a-wanda.author: Parent of two campers
camp-chen-a-wanda.text: Great spirit and strong athletics. Facilities are solid rather than fancy, and the price with extras adds up, but our kids count down the days all year.

camp-netimus.author: Mother of a shy first-timer
camp-netimus.text: Netimus was made for girls who don't fit the loud sporty camp mold. My daughter picked her own schedule, rode horses daily, and came home more confident. Cabins are rustic but that's the charm.

camp-netimus.author: Former camper, now in college
camp-netimus.text: Zero drama, all woodsmoke and lake swims. The elective system means you never dread an activity. Wish the sessions were longer honestly.

camp-shohola.author: Dad of a returning camper
camp-shohola.text: Shohola treats boys like capable people. My son learned to sail, shoot archery, and fix a bike in one month. Small enough that every counselor knew his name by day two.

camp-shohola.author: Alum from the 90s
camp-shohola.text: The elective system was ahead of its time and still is. It's rustic and low-key rather than flashy, which suits some kids better than others.

camp-cayuga.author: Parent of a first-time camper
camp-cayuga.text: The two-week option was a perfect trial run and the activity list is enormous. It's a big camp, so quieter kids need a nudge to find their group, but staff communication was good.

camp-cayuga.author: Camper, age 13
camp-cayuga.text: You literally pick your own schedule. I did paintball, waterskiing and horses in the same week. Food is decent, bunks get crowded.

camps-equinunk-blue-ridge.author: Third-generation camp parent
camps-equinunk-blue-ridge.text: My mother went, I went, now my kids go. Tradition is the whole point at CEBR - sing, banquet, the works. Not the camp for a family that wants short sessions.

camps-equinunk-blue-ridge.author: Alum, ten summers
camps-equinunk-blue-ridge.text: The brother-sister setup is special, and the friendships are lifelong. Facilities have improved but still lag the flashiest Poconos camps for the price.

camp-timber-tops.author: Mom of two TT girls
camp-timber-tops.text: The sisterhood thing is real - my daughters talk about their bunk like family. Communication from the office is excellent. Pricey, and the summer fills fast.

camp-timber-tops.author: Alum and former counselor
camp-timber-tops.text: Warm, well-run, tons of tradition. Some summers feel a little cliquey among returning campers, but staff work hard to fold new girls in.

lake-owego-camp.author: Parent of a nine-year-old
lake-owego-camp.text: Our son came home singing camp songs and asking to go back the next day. Counselors clearly love the place - many were campers themselves.

lake-owego-camp.author: Alum
lake-owego-camp.text: Great lake, great friends, sports all day. The bunks are old-school wood cabins - some kids love it, some miss AC.

camp-lake-greeley.author: Parent of a returning camper
camp-lake-greeley.text: Flexible sessions were the selling point for us and the waterfront is genuinely great. A few activities felt understaffed at peak season, but our kid had a blast.

camp-lake-greeley.author: Camper, age 12
camp-lake-greeley.text: The lake is the best part - banana boat, tubing, the blob. Evening activities are fun and the counselors are hilarious.

camp-susquehannock.author: Third-generation Susquehannock parent
camp-susquehannock.text: This place teaches sports the right way - effort and sportsmanship over winning. Same fields, same values for a century. Don't expect climbing walls and flat screens.

camp-susquehannock.author: Alum from the 80s
camp-susquehannock.text: Still get chills thinking about evening campfires over the lake. Rustic in a way that has almost disappeared from the camp world.

camp-westmont.author: Parent from Long Island
camp-westmont.text: Facilities rival the famous names at a slightly friendlier price. Our daughter loved the dance program and the lake. Buses and communication were smooth.

camp-westmont.author: Former counselor
camp-westmont.text: High-energy camp with a big international staff. Days are packed - great for social kids, a lot for kids who need downtime.

indian-head-camp.author: Parent of returning campers
indian-head-camp.text: Beautiful grounds, professional staff, and traditions the kids genuinely care about. Worth the money for us, though it is a lot of money.

indian-head-camp.author: Alum
indian-head-camp.text: Olympics at the end of summer was the highlight of my childhood. Food was hit or miss, friendships were not.

camp-oneka.author: Grandmother and Oneka alum
camp-oneka.text: Three generations of our family have worn Oneka whites. Small, kind, and timeless - girls learn to paddle, sail, and look out for each other.

camp-oneka.author: Parent of a first-timer
camp-oneka.text: The directors knew my daughter's name before she arrived. Old wooden cabins, cold lake mornings, zero phones - exactly what we wanted.

camp-tioga.author: Parent of two campers
camp-tioga.text: The directors are hands-on and it shows - our kids felt known. Beautiful lakefront, great mix of activities, and the half-summer option fits our family.

camp-tioga.author: Camper, age 14
camp-tioga.text: Amazing friends and the lake activities are top tier. Some of the sports courts are showing age but nobody really cares.

camp-lindenmere.author: Parent of a musician
camp-lindenmere.text: My son spent half his day in the recording studio and nobody made him play soccer. Total choice is the whole model. Downside: kids who need structure can drift.

camp-lindenmere.author: Camper, age 15
camp-lindenmere.text: You pick everything. I did aerial silks, guitar and skate park in one day. Staff treat you like a person, not a schedule.

camp-choconut.author: Father of a screen-loving son
camp-choconut.text: Sixty-some boys, real tools, real responsibility. My son built a lean-to and came home different - calmer, prouder. This is camp as it was in 1950, on purpose.

camp-choconut.author: Alum
camp-choconut.text: Choconut taught me to swing an axe and get over homesickness. Not for boys who need comfort - cold lake, lanterns at night - but unforgettable.

camp-pocono-trails.author: Parent of a teen
camp-pocono-trails.text: First place my son wasn't the last kid picked. Kind staff, real nutrition coaching, and he asked to go back. Results depend on follow-through at home.

camp-pocono-trails.author: Former camper
camp-pocono-trails.text: Nobody teases anyone about their body here, which was new for me. Food is portioned and healthy - you get used to it. Made friends who got it.

stone-mountain-adventures.author: Parent of a 14-year-old
stone-mountain-adventures.text: Teens-only was the magic - no little kids, real adventures, and staff who talk to campers like adults. My daughter wakeboarded, climbed, and put her phone away without complaint.

stone-mountain-adventures.author: Two-summer camper
stone-mountain-adventures.text: Only about 60 kids so everyone actually knows everyone. Raystown Lake days are unbeatable. Wish sessions were longer.

longacre-leadership-camp.author: Parent of an introvert
longacre-leadership-camp.text: Longacre asks kids to actually run things - meals, meetings, farm chores - and mine rose to it. Came home more articulate and confident. It's a farm, so expect dirt.

longacre-leadership-camp.author: Alum
longacre-leadership-camp.text: The community-meeting culture teaches you to speak up and listen. Less classic-camp glitz, more growing up. Loved the animals.

camp-kweebec.author: Philly-area parent
camp-kweebec.text: Being 45 minutes from home made the leap to sleepaway easy for all of us. Classic program, spirited staff. Grounds are nice if not glamorous.

camp-kweebec.author: Alum
camp-kweebec.text: Generations of my family did Kweebec summers. Color war and campfires exactly as you'd hope. Some cabins could use updating.

camp-saginaw.author: Parent of two returning campers
camp-saginaw.text: Saginaw is old-school in the best way - same families for generations, owners on site, kids outside all day. Less flashy than the Poconos camps and a bit gentler on the wallet.

camp-saginaw.author: Alum from Baltimore
camp-saginaw.text: Olympics, the canteen, cookouts - all the classics. Facilities are decent but it's the traditions that keep families coming back.

camp-green-lane.author: Second-generation CGL parent
camp-green-lane.text: I went, my kids go, and the songs haven't changed. It's close to Philly, the owners know every kid, and the community is everything.

camp-green-lane.author: Camper, age 12
camp-green-lane.text: Best friends I have are from camp. The pool and hockey rink are great. Food's just okay.

camp-onas.author: Parent of a nervous 8-year-old
camp-onas.text: Onas is kindness bootcamp. Silent meeting, cooperative games, counselors who de-escalate everything. And the price is almost unheard of for overnight camp.

camp-onas.author: Alum
camp-onas.text: No trophies, no cuts, no cliques that stuck. Just creeks, capture the flag and singing. The Quaker quiet grows on you.

camp-victory-pa.author: Parent of a child with type 1 diabetes
camp-victory-pa.text: The one week a year my son is just a regular kid, because every kid there checks blood sugar too. Medical staff everywhere, worry nowhere. We're so grateful this place exists.

camp-victory-pa.author: Volunteer counselor
camp-victory-pa.text: Each week serves a different community and the staff adapt everything. Facilities are simple but accessible. The joy is off the charts.

camp-poyntelle.author: Queens parent
camp-poyntelle.text: Feels like a big extended family - Friday night white shirts on the lake lawn gets me every summer. More affordable than most Poconos camps and the kids don't know the difference.

camp-poyntelle.author: Alum
camp-poyntelle.text: The lake and the friendships were everything. Facilities are older and it's not a luxury camp, but the ruach is real.

bnai-brith-perlman-camp.author: Parent of two campers
bnai-brith-perlman-camp.text: Pluralistic actually means it here - kids from Reform, Conservative and secular homes all fit. Israeli counselors bring incredible energy. Bunks are basic but clean.

bnai-brith-perlman-camp.author: Alum and BBYO kid
bnai-brith-perlman-camp.text: Perlman summers shaped my Jewish identity more than a decade of Hebrew school. Song sessions until your voice gives out.

camp-moshava-pa.author: Modern Orthodox parent
camp-moshava-pa.text: Mosh is a way of life in our community. Davening, Ivrit, mud football - our kids come home exhausted and glowing. Facilities are dated; nobody cares.

camp-moshava-pa.author: Alum
camp-moshava-pa.text: Shabbat at Moshava is something you can't explain to someone who hasn't stood in that chadar singing. Lifelong friends, many now in Israel.

camp-nesher.author: Parent from Bergen County
camp-nesher.text: Nesher nails the balance - serious Torah environment without feeling strict or joyless. The kids' counselors were role models in the best sense.

camp-nesher.author: Camper, age 13
camp-nesher.text: Great sports leagues and the lake is fun. Food is standard camp kosher, some days better than others.

camp-dora-golding.author: Father of three CDG boys
camp-dora-golding.text: Learning groups in the morning, hardball leagues in the afternoon - my sons live for it. The staff genuinely care about each boy's growth, ruchniyus and all.

camp-dora-golding.author: Alum
camp-dora-golding.text: Leagues were intense in the best way. Bunkhouses are old and summers are hot, but I'd trade a lot to do one more color war there.

golden-slipper-camp.author: Philadelphia parent
golden-slipper-camp.text: We could never afford the fancy Poconos camps - Golden Slipper made camp possible with real scholarship help, and my daughter got the same magic. Forever grateful.

golden-slipper-camp.author: Alum
golden-slipper-camp.text: Camp was the highlight of my childhood and I know my parents paid almost nothing. Facilities were tired even then, but the counselors made it feel rich.

pinemere-camp.author: Parent of a first-time camper
pinemere-camp.text: Pinemere took our anxious 8-year-old and gave us back a kid begging for four weeks next year. Inclusion staff checked in without hovering. Genuinely warm place.

pinemere-camp.author: Alum
pinemere-camp.text: Not the fanciest camp in the Poconos but easily one of the friendliest. Song sessions and Shabbat walks are what I remember most.

camp-havaya.author: Parent of a nonbinary camper
camp-havaya.text: The only camp where we didn't have to brief the staff about our kid - they were already ahead of us. Inclusion isn't a program here, it's the DNA.

camp-havaya.author: Camper, age 14
camp-havaya.text: Nobody performs cool at Havaya. You can be a theater kid, a Torah nerd and a gaga champion in the same day. Food could be better.

camp-galil.author: Parent of two Galilniks
camp-galil.text: Campers do real work - dishes, garden, planning programs - and they love it. It's kibbutz-style community, not country club. My kids came home more capable and more opinionated.

camp-galil.author: Alum
camp-galil.text: Galil taught me leadership by actually handing me responsibility at 16. Scrappy facilities, enormous heart, friends for life.

camp-nah-jee-wah.author: Parent of a 7-year-old
camp-nah-jee-wah.text: Perfect on-ramp to sleepaway - staff are pros with homesickness and the two-week starter session sealed the deal. He's already asking about Cedar Lake.

camp-nah-jee-wah.author: Former counselor
camp-nah-jee-wah.text: Sweet camp with tons of resources on a huge shared campus. It's big, so it can feel busy, but bunk staff keep the little ones' world small and safe.

cedar-lake-camp.author: Parent from Essex County
cedar-lake-camp.text: Our son moved up from Nah-Jee-Wah and the transition was seamless. Great leagues and trips. It's a large operation, so self-starters thrive most.

cedar-lake-camp.author: Alum
cedar-lake-camp.text: Cedar Lake summers were the glue of my teenage years. Shabbat by the lake with hundreds of kids singing - unbeatable.

capital-camps.author: DC-area parent
capital-camps.text: Basically everyone we know from synagogue sends kids here, and there's a reason - polished facilities, caring staff, and real inclusion support for our son's ADHD.

capital-camps.author: Camper, age 12
capital-camps.text: The lake and the adventure park are awesome. Song sessions get loud in the best way. Wish sessions were a little longer.

ymca-camp-speers.author: Parent of a first-timer
ymca-camp-speers.text: One week, under a thousand dollars with financial aid, and our daughter came home taller somehow. Y values without heavy religion. Cabins are rustic.

ymca-camp-speers.author: Alum
ymca-camp-speers.text: The lake is gorgeous and the staff culture is wholesome. Food is standard camp fare and some buildings are showing their age.

ymca-camp-fitch.author: Parent from Erie
ymca-camp-fitch.text: Fitch is an institution around here - I went, my kids go. Riding program is outstanding for a Y camp and the Lake Erie sunsets are unreal.

ymca-camp-fitch.author: Longtime counselor
ymca-camp-fitch.text: Staff come back year after year, some for decades. The traditions and the bluff over the lake make it feel bigger than a summer job.

ymca-camp-kon-o-kwee-spencer.author: Pittsburgh parent
ymca-camp-kon-o-kwee-spencer.text: Every kid in our neighborhood has done a week at Konokwee. Affordable, well-run, and the counselors bring huge energy. Sign up early - weeks fill fast.

ymca-camp-kon-o-kwee-spencer.author: Alum
ymca-camp-kon-o-kwee-spencer.text: Chapel on the hill, creek hikes, camp songs - simple and great. Bathhouses were dated when I went, though I hear they've upgraded.

ymca-camp-soles.author: Parent of two campers
ymca-camp-soles.text: Small enough that the director greeted my kids by name on day one. Beautiful lake, unplugged week, honest price. It's rustic - pack for it.

ymca-camp-soles.author: Alum
ymca-camp-soles.text: Laurel Highlands setting is gorgeous and the vibe is friendly, not cliquey. Rainy weeks can drag since indoor space is limited.

ymca-camp-shady-brook.author: Central PA parent
ymca-camp-shady-brook.text: Solid, affordable week of classic camp. Our son loved the creek hikes and gaga. Communication was a little thin mid-week, but he came home happy and filthy, as intended.

ymca-camp-shady-brook.author: Camper, age 11
ymca-camp-shady-brook.text: The pool parties and campout night were my favorite. Counselors are really funny. Bring bug spray.

camp-shand.author: Lancaster parent
camp-shand.text: The Y made sure cost was never a barrier - most kids there get help. My daughter got the same campfires and pool games as any pricey camp. Big-hearted place.

camp-shand.author: Alum
camp-shand.text: Camp Shand gave a lot of us our only week away as kids. Nothing fancy, everything that matters.

camp-conrad-weiser.author: Berks County parent
camp-conrad-weiser.text: Great value and a beautiful mountain campus. Our kids loved the pool and the overnight campout. Some cabins are old, but the program is well organized.

camp-conrad-weiser.author: Former counselor
camp-conrad-weiser.text: The staff community is tight and the campus is huge - trails everywhere. Kids from all backgrounds mix easily here.

camp-ladore.author: Parent from Scranton
camp-ladore.text: Our church helped send the kids and Ladore treated them like royalty - boating, crafts, three big meals. For families like ours this camp is a blessing, full stop.

camp-ladore.author: Former camper
camp-ladore.text: There's chapel and grace before meals, but mostly I remember the giant lake and feeling safe. Dorm-style bunks are crowded and loud - part of the fun.

camp-swatara.author: Brethren parent
camp-swatara.text: Three generations of our family have camped at Swatara. Gentle faith, muddy creeks, no screens. Exactly what childhood should include.

camp-swatara.author: Former camper
camp-swatara.text: Loved the pool and the AT hikes. It's simple - don't expect zip lines everywhere - but the community feeling is rare.

bear-creek-camp.author: Lutheran parent
bear-creek-camp.text: The counselors' enthusiasm is contagious - our kids came home singing grace loudly and asking to go for two weeks next year. Gorgeous, wild property.

bear-creek-camp.author: Alum
bear-creek-camp.text: Mountain hikes, the lake, silly all-camp games. Very outdoorsy - if your kid hates bugs and hills, maybe not the one.

gretna-glen-camp.author: Parent of two campers
gretna-glen-camp.text: Sweet, well-run week - counselors sent postcards home mid-session. Faith content is warm, not heavy. The Gretna woods are magical.

gretna-glen-camp.author: Former camper
gretna-glen-camp.text: Creek stomping and campfire worship are what I remember. Small camp, everyone knows you by Tuesday.

pocono-plateau-camp.author: Parent from the Lehigh Valley
pocono-plateau-camp.text: A fraction of the price of the famous Poconos camps and my son got the same lake mornings and campfires, plus counselors who prayed with him when he was homesick.

pocono-plateau-camp.author: Alum
pocono-plateau-camp.text: Peaceful, woodsy, and friendly. Activities are classic rather than flashy - canoes over wakeboards.

camp-men-o-lan.author: Bucks County parent
camp-men-o-lan.text: Kind counselors, good food, and a price that let us send all three kids. Chapel is upbeat and the pool is the center of the day.

camp-men-o-lan.author: Alum
camp-men-o-lan.text: I did a decade of summers there. Simple cabins, big friendships, faith that felt real and unforced.

pinebrook-bible-camp.author: Parent of returning campers
pinebrook-bible-camp.text: Solid Bible teaching and counselors who invest in the kids. The schedule is full - chapel, games, pool, repeat - and my boys thrive on it.

pinebrook-bible-camp.author: Alum
pinebrook-bible-camp.text: Made my closest church friends there. It's old-fashioned and proud of it; the facilities match the price.

camp-susque.author: Williamsport-area parent
camp-susque.text: No cell service, cold creek, counselors who know every camper - Susque is the real thing. My kids talk about it all year.

camp-susque.author: Alum
camp-susque.text: Learning to build a fire in the rain at Susque is still one of my proudest childhood memories. Tiny camp, huge impact.

krislund-camp.author: State College parent
krislund-camp.text: Our kids' favorite week of the year - zip line, mud games, campfire worship. Staff handle first-timers with real care.

krislund-camp.author: Former counselor
krislund-camp.text: The valley setting is gorgeous and the community is tight. Facilities are functional rather than fancy, and that's fine.

camp-blue-diamond.author: Parent of two campers
camp-blue-diamond.text: Surrounded by forest, no phones, counselors who model kindness. My kids come home calmer. It's rustic - lantern-lit rustic - and we love it.

camp-blue-diamond.author: Alum
camp-blue-diamond.text: Mountain hikes and the lake were highlights. Quiet camp with a strong sense of peace and service.

camp-sequanota.author: Johnstown-area parent
camp-sequanota.text: Counselors radiate patience and joy. Our son with mild autism was welcomed without a fuss. Simple mountain camp done right.

camp-sequanota.author: Alum
camp-sequanota.text: Great memories of campfires and the mud pit games. Food was homey. Some buildings need love.

camp-harmony-pa.author: Somerset County parent
camp-harmony-pa.text: Nothing fancy, everything wholesome - creek time, crafts, singing. The price means every kid in our congregation can go.

camp-harmony-pa.author: Alum
camp-harmony-pa.text: Camp Harmony summers shaped my faith more than Sunday school ever did. Cabins are drafty, hearts are warm.

camp-allegheny-pa.author: Parent of a returning camper
camp-allegheny-pa.text: Strong Bible teaching, horses, and counselors who follow up with campers during the year. Our daughter asks to go back before we've left the parking lot.

camp-allegheny-pa.author: Alum
camp-allegheny-pa.text: High-energy chapel, muddy games, decent food. It's unapologetically evangelistic, so know that going in.

jumonville-camp.author: Uniontown parent
jumonville-camp.text: Hiking to the Cross at sunrise is a rite of passage here. Programs are well-organized and the mountaintop views never get old.

jumonville-camp.author: Alum
jumonville-camp.text: Went every summer as a kid - sports camps were great and worship nights meant something. Mountain weather keeps you guessing.

ligonier-camp.author: Pittsburgh parent
ligonier-camp.text: The alpine tower and creek hikes had our son hooked, and the counselors' faith is genuine without being heavy-handed. Well run top to bottom.

ligonier-camp.author: Former counselor
ligonier-camp.text: Staff training is excellent and the traditions run deep. Kids leave more confident than they came - that's the whole point.

laurelville-camp.author: Parent of an artsy camper
laurelville-camp.text: Singing, crafts, creek walks, and an ethic of kindness you can feel. Not a sports camp, and that's why we chose it.

laurelville-camp.author: Alum
laurelville-camp.text: Four-part hymn singing in the dining hall is a core memory. Facilities are simple retreat-style lodges.

camp-lutherlyn.author: Butler County parent
camp-lutherlyn.text: The horse program is legit - my daughter progressed from lead-line to trail rides over three summers. Faith formation is joyful, never guilt-based.

camp-lutherlyn.author: Alum
camp-lutherlyn.text: Lutherlyn is where I learned to love the woods. The traditions - banquet, vespers hill - stick with you for decades.

camp-crestfield.author: Parent of a hesitant camper
camp-crestfield.text: Counselor-to-camper ratio felt like family. They eased our anxious son in and by Thursday he'd forgotten to miss us. Simple and lovely.

camp-crestfield.author: Alum
camp-crestfield.text: Creek stomping and campfires - classic church camp done with care. Not much for a kid who wants big-time sports.

camp-orchard-hill.author: NEPA parent
camp-orchard-hill.text: Feels like a big production in the best way - themed weeks, hilarious staff skits, real conversations about faith. Our kids beg for two weeks.

camp-orchard-hill.author: Camper, age 13
camp-orchard-hill.text: Paintball and the lake toys are awesome. Chapel is actually fun. Cabins get hot in July.

black-rock-retreat.author: Lancaster parent
black-rock-retreat.text: Beautiful hemlock woods, kind staff, and a week that cost less than a day at an amusement park per day. Our kids return every year.

black-rock-retreat.author: Alum
black-rock-retreat.text: The ropes course and creek were the highlights. Quiet, faith-filled, and unhurried - a nice contrast to school-year chaos.

heritage-reservation.author: Scoutmaster from Pittsburgh
heritage-reservation.text: First-class facilities by Scout camp standards - the lake program and COPE course are excellent. Proximity to Ohiopyle means great add-on adventures.

heritage-reservation.author: Scout, age 15
heritage-reservation.text: Sailing merit badge on a real lake, campfires with the whole reservation. Uphill hikes everywhere, but that's Scout camp.

camp-vacamas.author: Parent from Paterson
camp-vacamas.text: Vacamas gave my son a real sleepaway summer we couldn't otherwise afford. Diverse campers, caring staff, beautiful lake. This place changes lives quietly.

camp-vacamas.author: Alum
camp-vacamas.text: Kids from every background sharing a bunk - that was the education. Facilities are older but the traditions are strong.

fairview-lake-ymca-camp.author: Parent of two campers
fairview-lake-ymca-camp.text: The lake is spectacular and the staff run a tight, warm ship. Y pricing plus financial aid made a month possible. Cabins are simple - kids don't notice.

fairview-lake-ymca-camp.author: Alum from the 90s
fairview-lake-ymca-camp.text: Sailing on Fairview Lake at 10 years old is still one of my favorite memories. Food was mediocre then and I hear it's improved.

ymca-camp-mason.author: Parent of a returning camper
ymca-camp-mason.text: Mason's culture is the selling point - kids from a dozen countries, zero snobbery. The skate park and horses sealed it for ours. Communication is excellent.

ymca-camp-mason.author: Former counselor
ymca-camp-mason.text: Genuinely values-driven place to work. Campers get real independence and the Water Gap setting is gorgeous. Rainy weeks test the indoor spaces.

ymca-camp-bernie.author: First-time camp parent
ymca-camp-bernie.text: Perfect starter camp - close to home, one-week sessions, counselors who called when our daughter had a wobbly first night. She finished the week beaming.

ymca-camp-bernie.author: Camper, age 11
ymca-camp-bernie.text: Horseback riding and the giant swing were my favorites. The pond is small but the pool makes up for it.

camp-ockanickon.author: South Jersey dad and alum
camp-ockanickon.text: Canoeing the cedar water at dusk is something every boy should do once. My son's counselors were superb. Rustic camp, deliberately so.

camp-ockanickon.author: Camper, age 12
camp-ockanickon.text: The lake looks like iced tea and it's awesome. Polar bear swim is freezing. Food is okay, tacos are the best day.

camp-matollionequay.author: Mom of a Matollionequay lifer
camp-matollionequay.text: Five summers in and the counselors still remember her from year one. Sister-brother camp setup is handy for siblings. Bug spray is non-negotiable in the Pines.

camp-matollionequay.author: Alum
camp-matollionequay.text: Campfire songs across Lake Stockwell, riding in the pines - lovely and low-key. Cabins are old wooden platforms, part of the charm.

camp-dark-waters.author: Quaker parent
camp-dark-waters.text: Silent grace, cedar water swims, kids of every kind welcomed exactly as they are. Dark Waters is small on purpose and better for it.

camp-dark-waters.author: Alum
camp-dark-waters.text: The creek, the pines, the kindness - I didn't realize how rare that culture was until I grew up. Bring a flashlight and a sense of humor about mosquitoes.

camp-louemma.author: North Jersey parent
camp-louemma.text: The owners are on site and know every kid. Two-week option let us test the waters; now it's a month every summer. Classic camp without the classic sticker shock.

camp-louemma.author: Alum
camp-louemma.text: Color war, talent shows, lake days - all the staples done warmly. Grounds are tidy if not luxurious.

cross-roads-camp-nj.author: Episcopal parent
cross-roads-camp-nj.text: Inclusive, gentle Christianity and counselors trained to meet kids where they are. Our daughter's confidence grew visibly in one week.

cross-roads-camp-nj.author: Former camper
cross-roads-camp-nj.text: Small camp where nobody slips through the cracks. Pond swimming and campfires over flash - which was right for me.

aldersgate-center-nj.author: Methodist parent
aldersgate-center-nj.text: Lovely woods, sincere staff, honest price. Programming is simple - hikes, swim time, chapel - and my kids were happily worn out.

aldersgate-center-nj.author: Alum
aldersgate-center-nj.text: My favorite week of every childhood summer. Swartswood sunsets from the waterfront are still my mental screensaver.

camp-tecumseh-nj.author: Newark parent
camp-tecumseh-nj.text: Our church sponsored both my kids and Tecumseh treated them beautifully - swimming every day, kind counselors, real meals. A godsend for working families.

camp-tecumseh-nj.author: Former camper
camp-tecumseh-nj.text: First place I ever swam in a real pool. Chapel every day, but mostly I remember feeling free. Dorms were loud at night.

camp-merry-heart.author: Parent of a camper with cerebral palsy
camp-merry-heart.text: Merry Heart is the one week my daughter is a camper first and a diagnosis second. Adapted everything, nurses on site, and she comes home singing.

camp-merry-heart.author: Longtime counselor
camp-merry-heart.text: The joy at this camp humbles you. Facilities are aging and fundraising never stops, but the care is world-class.

trail-blazers-camp.author: Brooklyn parent
trail-blazers-camp.text: My city kid cooked dinner over a fire she built. Groups are tiny - eight kids, two counselors - so nobody disappears. Scholarships made it possible.

trail-blazers-camp.author: Alum
trail-blazers-camp.text: No bunks with outlets, just woods and your crew. Hard some days, formative always. I still smell campfire and grin.

lindley-g-cook-4h-camp.author: Sussex County parent
lindley-g-cook-4h-camp.text: Down-to-earth camp where kids catch frogs, shoot archery, and unplug completely. You don't need to be in 4-H, and the price is right.

lindley-g-cook-4h-camp.author: Camper, age 12
lindley-g-cook-4h-camp.text: The lake and the nature hikes were great and I made friends fast. Cabins are pretty basic but the campfires are the best part.

camp-mystic-tx.author: Third-generation camp family
camp-mystic-tx.text: Mystic is woven into our family history. The tribe traditions, the river, the counselors who return year after year — it's a place girls carry with them for life. Getting a spot requires signing up years ahead.

camp-mystic-tx.author: Alum from the 2000s
camp-mystic-tx.text: The friendships I made on that river are still my closest ones. It's old-fashioned in the best way — no frills, lots of tradition. Not the camp for a kid who wants modern facilities.

camp-waldemar.author: Mother of two campers
camp-waldemar.text: The grounds are stunning and the traditions are taken seriously — table manners included. My daughters count down the days all year. Be warned: legacy families dominate enrollment, so plan very early.

camp-waldemar.author: Former counselor
camp-waldemar.text: Beautiful, well-run, and steeped in ritual. Some girls thrive on the tribe competition; a few find the social hierarchy intense. Food is genuinely excellent.

camp-stewart-for-boys.author: Dad of a returning camper
camp-stewart-for-boys.text: Stewart turned my screen-obsessed son into a kid who begs to go back to the river. Great mix of competition and tradition. Cabins are rustic — that's the point.

camp-stewart-for-boys.author: Alum and former staffer
camp-stewart-for-boys.text: A hundred years of tradition and it shows, mostly for the better. Days are structured and busy. Texas summer heat is no joke, but the river makes up for it.

heart-o-the-hills-camp.author: Parent of a first-time camper
heart-o-the-hills-camp.text: Smaller than the famous neighbors and that's its superpower — my shy daughter was known by name on day one. The riding program is the highlight.

camp-rio-vista.author: Parent of two boys
camp-rio-vista.text: A hundred years of practice shows — the program runs like clockwork and the staff culture is strong. My boys love the river and the ranger program.

camp-rio-vista.author: Alum
camp-rio-vista.text: Classic old Texas camp. Facilities are dated in places, but the traditions and the creek swimming are exactly what camp should be.

camp-sierra-vista.author: Parent of a returning camper
camp-sierra-vista.text: Our daughter attends while her brother is at Rio Vista next door — same closing ceremony, one pickup. Warm staff and great flexibility with shorter sessions.

camp-la-junta.author: Mom of a non-sporty kid
camp-la-junta.text: La Junta's choose-your-own-schedule approach was perfect for my son who hates forced competition. He came home confident and sunburned in the best way.

camp-la-junta.author: Alum
camp-la-junta.text: The freedom is real — you build your own day. Less rah-rah than other Hunt camps, which suited me. Cabins are simple and hot in July.

camp-honey-creek.author: Parent of a returning camper
camp-honey-creek.text: Honey Creek feels like family — the directors greet returning girls by name and the tribe rivalry is spirited but kind. Less flashy than its neighbors, and we like it that way.

kickapoo-kamp.author: Alum whose daughter now attends
kickapoo-kamp.text: Almost nothing has changed since I went in the 80s, and that's exactly why we chose it. Rustic bunks, horses every day, zero pretension. Not for girls who need air conditioning.

camp-lonehollow.author: Parent of two campers
camp-lonehollow.text: Facilities are honestly nicer than some resorts — climbing walls, gorgeous dining hall, spring-fed water. Kids pick their own activities and love it. Price reflects the polish.

camp-lonehollow.author: Camper, age 14
camp-lonehollow.text: So many activities you can't do them all. Cabins are amazing. It's newer so it doesn't have the deep traditions my friends' camps have, but we're kind of making them.

camp-balcones-springs.author: Houston parent
camp-balcones-springs.text: Beautifully run and very social — my kids came home hoarse from cheering. Communication with parents is excellent. It's pricey and books up early.

camp-balcones-springs.author: Camper, age 12
camp-balcones-springs.text: The lake blob and ski boats are the best part. Spirit competitions are constant, which I mostly love but it can be a lot.

camp-peniel.author: Parent of a returning camper
camp-peniel.text: Unapologetically old-school Bible camp — solid teaching, sweet counselors, creek swimming. Facilities are basic but everything important is done well.

camp-of-the-hills.author: Camper sponsored through a church
camp-of-the-hills.text: I never thought I'd get to go to a camp like this. The counselors treated us like family and I rode a horse for the first time.

camp-young-judaea-texas.author: Houston Jewish parent
camp-young-judaea-texas.text: CYJ is where Texas Jewish kids find their people. The Shabbat song sessions give me chills at pickup. Facilities are decent, not fancy — the community is the product.

camp-young-judaea-texas.author: Alum
camp-young-judaea-texas.text: Lifelong friends, Israeli dance, brutal Texas heat. The pool schedule saves you. Wish sessions were a bit cheaper, but scholarships helped.

rocky-river-ranch.author: Parent of a horse-crazy 8-year-old
rocky-river-ranch.text: Daily riding, warm counselors, and Cypress Creek swimming — my daughter cried at pickup because she didn't want to leave. Small and sells out quickly.

camp-el-tesoro.author: Fort Worth parent
camp-el-tesoro.text: El Tesoro meets kids where they are — my anxious son was supported without being singled out. Great value compared to the private camps.

camp-el-tesoro.author: Counselor
camp-el-tesoro.text: Genuinely inclusive place with a long history. Facilities show their age here and there, but the horse program and river days are wonderful.

ymca-camp-grady-spruce.author: Dallas parent
ymca-camp-grady-spruce.text: Great waterfront program at a price normal families can manage. Cabins are basic and it's a big operation, so it can feel less personal — but my kids adore it.

ymca-camp-grady-spruce.author: Alum
ymca-camp-grady-spruce.text: Learned to sail and water ski at Grady Spruce in the 90s and my kids do the same things off the same docks now. That lake at sunset is pure magic.

ymca-camp-flaming-arrow.author: San Antonio parent
ymca-camp-flaming-arrow.text: Same river as the fancy camps for a third of the cost. Program is solid and counselors are sweet. Don't expect gourmet food or new cabins.

camp-chrysalis-kerrville.author: Lutheran parent
camp-chrysalis-kerrville.text: Gentle, faith-filled week on a gorgeous stretch of river. Counselors set a kind tone and homesick kids get real care. Simple cabins, big hearts.

texas-lions-camp.author: Parent of a camper with type 1 diabetes
texas-lions-camp.text: Medical staff handle everything so my son is just a kid for a week — and it costs nothing. The confidence he brings home is priceless.

texas-lions-camp.author: Former counselor
texas-lions-camp.text: The 'it can be done' philosophy is real — every activity is adapted so every kid participates. Hands down the most meaningful summer job I've had.

camp-camp-texas.author: Parent of a camper with complex needs
camp-camp-texas.text: Camps kept turning us away until CAMP said yes. Nurses, one-on-one counselors, real camp activities adapted for my daughter — and respite for us. We're forever grateful.

john-knox-ranch.author: Austin parent
john-knox-ranch.text: The Blanco swimming hole is heaven and the culture is gentle and goofy in the best way. Cabins are screened and rustic — pack a fan.

highland-lakes-camp.author: Youth minister
highland-lakes-camp.text: Well-organized host camp — our group returns every June. Lake time is the highlight; dorm-style lodging is functional rather than charming.

camp-buckner-tx.author: Parent of a middle schooler
camp-buckner-tx.text: Great balance of adventure and faith — my son talks about his counselor's cabin devotionals as much as the lake toys. Registration fills fast.

alto-frio-baptist-camp.author: Youth group alum
alto-frio-baptist-camp.text: Decades of Texas Baptist kids have their faith stories rooted at Alto Frio. The Frio is cold and perfect. Lodging is bare-bones camp style — bring a good pillow.

camp-kachina.author: Central Texas Scout mom
camp-kachina.text: Solid, affordable outdoor camp — my daughter loves the horses and lake days. It's hot and rustic, and some units could use updating, but the staff spirit carries it.

camp-olympia.author: Houston parent
camp-olympia.text: Olympia nails the balance — real competition through the Trojan/Spartan rivalry but counselors keep it kind. Lake Livingston waterfront is huge. Book by fall for summer.

camp-olympia.author: Alum
camp-olympia.text: Went for eight summers. The war canoe races and Olympia Bowl are unforgettable. It's big, so quieter kids need a session to find their footing.

pine-cove-camps.author: Dallas parent
pine-cove-camps.text: The counselor energy is unmatched — my kids quote their nicknamed counselors all year. Solid biblical teaching without feeling heavy. Sessions sell out in hours in January.

pine-cove-camps.author: Former counselor
pine-cove-camps.text: Amazing staff community and intentional ministry. It's polished and programmatic — less free-range than old-school camps — and the week flies by fast.

camp-huawni.author: Parent of returning campers
camp-huawni.text: Huawni feels like summer at a beloved relative's lake place — banana boat rides, talent shows, counselors who remember your kid's inside jokes from last year.

frontier-camp.author: East Texas parent
frontier-camp.text: Solid biblical camp with a beautiful piney lake. Our kids love the blob and the wranglers. Less flashy than the big-name camps and noticeably cheaper.

camp-coyote.author: Houston parent
camp-coyote.text: Real ranch feel an hour from Houston — my son fishes and rides all week. Cabins are simple and the schedule is relaxed. Great value.

echo-hill-ranch.author: Alum
echo-hill-ranch.text: Echo Hill is one of a kind — storytelling, swimming holes, and a warmth you can't manufacture. Facilities are genuinely rustic; that's part of the deal.

camp-fern.author: Louisiana parent
camp-fern.text: Fern draws from East Texas, Dallas, and Louisiana and the loyalty is fierce. The lake with its cypress trees is stunning. Traditions run deep — new families should expect to learn the ropes.

ymca-camp-carter.author: Fort Worth parent
ymca-camp-carter.text: Close to home, affordable, and my kids beg to return for the horse program. It's a busy Y camp so expect some chaos at drop-off and simple food.

ymca-camp-cullen.author: Houston parent
ymca-camp-cullen.text: Impressive facilities for a Y camp — air-conditioned cabins were a relief in the Texas heat. Sailing program is legit. Sessions with popular weeks fill early.

camp-tejas.author: Youth pastor
camp-tejas.text: The staff serve like it's their calling — our group is treated wonderfully every year. Grounds are tidy and the lake activities keep every kid busy.

camp-blessing-texas.author: Parent of a camper with Down syndrome
camp-blessing-texas.text: One-on-one buddies, real camp adventures, and a sibling week so our other kids get camp too. We count down to it all year.

camp-for-all.author: Parent of a camper with epilepsy
camp-for-all.text: Every single activity is adapted so no kid watches from the sidelines. Medical staff on site gave us total peace of mind. A truly special place.

camp-summit-tx.author: Parent of a camper who uses a wheelchair
camp-summit-tx.text: My daughter rode a horse and slept away from home for the first time at 12. The staff treat every camper with dignity. Sessions fill quickly for high-support weeks.

camp-aranzazu.author: Nonprofit group leader
camp-aranzazu.text: The bayfront setting sets it apart — our kids kayak and fish where herons wade. Facilities are modern and truly accessible, not retrofitted as an afterthought.

greene-family-camp.author: Dallas parent
greene-family-camp.text: GFC gave our kids Jewish joy you can't get in Sunday school — they come home singing blessings. Inclusive of all kinds of families. Central Texas heat is the only complaint.

greene-family-camp.author: Alum
greene-family-camp.text: Shabbat under the big Texas sky with 300 friends singing — still gives me chills. Staff took real care of first-timers.

camp-doublecreek.author: Round Rock parent
camp-doublecreek.text: Horseback riding at a day camp is a rare find. Buses make logistics easy and the end-of-week rodeo shows are adorable. Summer weeks book by early spring.

camp-thurman.author: Arlington parent
camp-thurman.text: The counselor energy is legendary — chants, skits, face paint, all of it. My kids don't even realize it's faith-based mentoring. Spots vanish fast.

camp-hoblitzelle.author: Grandmother of two campers
camp-hoblitzelle.text: My grandkids would never afford camp otherwise. They swim, ride paddleboats, and come home standing taller. The Salvation Army does this right.

sea-camp-galveston.author: Parent of a future marine biologist
sea-camp-galveston.text: Real field science — boat days, dolphin surveys, lab work with actual researchers. Dorm life is a fun preview of college. It's structured learning, not campfires and s'mores.

camp-lantern-creek.author: Parent of a creative kid
